theorem:
\label{ergodic-th}
If Assumption B holds, then $\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in \mathbb{R}}$ is ergodic, for any $t>0$. Consequently, by the mean ergodic theorem,
\[
\frac{1}{R}F_R(t) \to 0 \quad \mbox{a.s and in $L^2(\Omega)$}, \quad \mbox{as $R \to \infty$}.
\]

mcq.question:
Let \(u\) be the solution to the one-dimensional stochastic wave equation
\[
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}(t,x)=\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}(t,x)+u(t,x)\,\dot X(t,x),\qquad t>0,\ x\in\mathbb R,
\]
with initial conditions \(u(0,x)=1\) and \(\partial_t u(0,x)=0\), where \(X\) is the Lévy colored noise from the setup. Suppose Assumption B holds, and let \(x\mapsto u(t,x)\) denote the spatial random field at a fixed time \(t>0\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the field \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb R}\) for every \(t>0\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is ergodic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is strongly mixing under spatial shifts, since the one-dimensional wave kernel has finite propagation speed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is stationary with respect to spatial translations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For sufficiently large times \(t\), but not necessarily for all \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) becomes ergodic because the wave kernel averages the noise over larger spatial regions.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every fixed \(t>0\), the spatial process \(\{u(t,x)\}_{x\in\mathbb{R}}\) is ergodic, and moreover \(R^{-1}F_R(t)\to 0\) almost surely and in \(L^2(\Omega)\) uniformly for \(t\) in every compact interval of \((0,\infty)\) as \(R\to\infty\).

sketch: To prove the main theorem, the authors state that they "employ convolution estimates, energy estimates, and an analysis of Riccati-type dynamics along characteristics" and that they "first overcome the technical challenges posed by the general nonlinearity to establish a blow-up criterion." They further indicate (in the paper roadmap) that the following part is dedicated to the proof of this blow-up criterion.

mcq.question:
Let \(g\in C^\infty(\mathbb R)\) with \(g(0)=0\). For \(s>0\), define
\[
X^s(\mathbb R^2):=\{f\in H^s(\mathbb R^2):\partial_x^{-1}f\in H^s(\mathbb R^2),\ \partial_x f\in H^s(\mathbb R^2)\},
\]
where \(\partial_x^{-1}f\) is the Fourier multiplier with symbol \((i\xi)^{-1}\). Let \(s\ge 2\), let \(u_0\in X^s(\mathbb R^2)\), and let
\[
 u\in C([0,T_{u_0}^*);X^s(\mathbb R^2))\cap C^1([0,T_{u_0}^*);X^{s-2}(\mathbb R^2))
\]
be the unique solution on its maximal existence interval \([0,T_{u_0}^*)\) of
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t+\gamma u u_x+G*\partial_x\left(\frac12 g(u)+\frac\gamma2 u_x^2-\frac\gamma2 u^2\right)+G*v_y=0,\quad (x,y)\in\mathbb R^2,\\
\nu_y=v_x,\\
u\big|_{t=0}=u_0(x,y),\quad (x,y)\in\mathbb R^2.
\end{cases}
\]
Assume that the maximal existence time is finite, \(T_{u_0}^*<\infty\). Writing \(\nabla u=(u_x,u_y)\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the blow-up behavior of \(u\) as \(t\to T_{u_0}^{*-}\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
Necessarily,
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|\nabla u(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}^2\,d\tau=\infty.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
Necessarily,
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|\nabla u(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}\,d\tau=\infty.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
Necessarily,
\[
\limsup_{t\to T_{u_0}^{* -}}\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
Necessarily, for every \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\),
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|\nabla u(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}^{2-\varepsilon}\,d\tau=\infty.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
Necessarily,
\[
\int_0^{T_{u_0}^*}\|u_x(\tau)\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^2)}^2\,d\tau=\infty.
\]

mcq.question:
Assume that \(g\in C_b^5\) (that is, \(g:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is \(C^5\) with bounded derivatives) and that the initial condition \(\psi\in C^\theta(\mathbb T^2)\) for some \(\theta\in(0,1/4)\). Let \(\rho:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\) be smooth, radial, nonnegative, supported on the unit ball, and such that \(\int_{\mathbb R^2}\rho=1\). Set \(\rho_\varepsilon(x)=\varepsilon^{-2}\rho(\varepsilon^{-1}x)\) and define \(\xi_\varepsilon=\rho_\varepsilon*\xi\). For each \(\varepsilon>0\), let \(u_\varepsilon\) be the solution on the two-dimensional torus \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\) of
\[
\partial_t u_\varepsilon = \Delta u_\varepsilon + g(u_\varepsilon)\,\varepsilon^{1/2}\,\partial_{x_1}\xi_\varepsilon - C_\varepsilon g'g(u_\varepsilon)-\bar C_\varepsilon (g')^3 g(u_\varepsilon)-\hat C_\varepsilon g''g'g^2(u_\varepsilon).
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the limit of \(u_\varepsilon\) as \(\varepsilon\to 0\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exist sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\) and constants \(c_\rho,\lambda_\rho>0\) such that \(u_\varepsilon\) converges in law as \(\varepsilon\to0\) in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\) to the renormalized solution \(u\) of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta-\lambda_\rho g''g'g^2(u)
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), where \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exist sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\) and a constant \(c_\rho>0\) such that for every sequence \(\varepsilon_n\to0\), one can extract a subsequence \(\varepsilon_{n_k}\to0\) for which \(u_{\varepsilon_{n_k}}\) converges in law in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\) to the renormalized solution \(u\) of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), where \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every admissible mollifier \(\rho\), the same sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\), independent of \(\rho\), can be chosen together with a constant \(c_\rho>0\) so that \(u_\varepsilon\) converges in law as \(\varepsilon\to0\) in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\) to the renormalized solution \(u\) of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), where \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exist sequences of constants \(C_\varepsilon,\bar C_\varepsilon,\hat C_\varepsilon\) and a constant \(c_\rho>0\) such that, with all \(u_\varepsilon\) constructed from the same underlying noise \(\xi\), one has \(u_\varepsilon\to u\) in probability in \(C([0,1]\times\mathbb T^2)\), where \(u\) is the renormalized solution of
\[
\partial_t u=\Delta u+c_\rho g'g(u)\,\eta
\]
on \(\mathbb T^2\) with initial condition \(\psi\), and \(\eta\) is a spatial white noise.

theorem:
[Local density of $\mathcal{S}$ in short intervals]\label{thm:main-elements}
Fix $\varepsilon>0$. Let $\theta$ satisfy $2/15+\varepsilon<\theta<1-\delta_0$ for some absolute constant $\delta_0>0$, and set $h=X^{\theta}$.
Then for all but $O_{\varepsilon}\left(X\exp\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)$ integers $x\in[X,2X]$, one has
\[
\#\left(\mathcal{S}\cap(x,x+h]\right)\ \asymp\ h.
\]
The implied constants depend at most on $\lambda$, $\varepsilon$ and $\theta$.
sketch:
“To access [Theorem~\ref{thm:main-elements}] through Cauchy--Schwarz,” the paper first proves a first-moment statement for the representation function $f$, namely Theorem~\ref{thm:main-rep} that $R(x)\asymp h$ for almost all $x\in[X,2X]$. For this, the “pointwise upper bound $R(x)\ll h$ follows from Brun--Titchmarsh together with $\#\cA_{\lambda}(X)\asymp\log X$,” while the “almost-all lower bound $R(x)\gg h$ is obtained by restricting to $a\leqslant h/2$ and applying the Guth--Maynard ... asymptotic for primes in almost all short intervals (with a stretched-exponential exceptional set).”

“To pass from representations to elements” (i.e. to deduce Theorem~\ref{thm:main-elements}), they “combine Cauchy--Schwarz, which relates $R(x)$, $S(x)$ and the local second moment $Q(x)$, with a uniform bound $Q(x)\ll h$.” This uniform second-moment bound “is proved using Selberg's upper-bound sieve for prime pairs together with Chen--Ding--Xu--Zhai's estimate controlling the contribution of small prime divisors in differences $a_1-a_2$ with $a_1,a_2\in\cA_{\lambda}(X)$.”

mcq.question:
Fix \(\varepsilon>0\). Let \(\theta\) satisfy \(2/15+\varepsilon<\theta<1-\delta_0\) for some absolute constant \(\delta_0>0\), and set \(h=X^{\theta}\). Let \(\mathbb P\) denote the set of prime numbers, and define
\[
\mathcal A_{\lambda}:=\left\{2^{\lfloor k_1^{r_1}\rfloor}+\cdots+2^{\lfloor k_{s-1}^{r_{s-1}}\rfloor}+2^{\lfloor \lfloor k_s^{\lambda}\rfloor^{r_s}\rfloor}:\ k_1,\dots,k_s\in\mathbb N\right\},
\]
with \(\mathcal A_{\lambda}(X):=\mathcal A_{\lambda}\cap[1,2X]\), and let
\[
\mathcal S:=\mathbb P+\mathcal A_{\lambda}(X).
\]
Write \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\) for the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in the interval \((x,x+h]\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\) for integers \(x\in[X,2X]\) as \(X\to\infty\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/3}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\); equivalently, the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in \((x,x+h]\) is bounded above and below by positive constant multiples of \(h\) outside an exceptional set of that size.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a set \(\mathcal E_X\subset [X,2X]\cap\mathbb Z\) with \(\#\mathcal E_X=o(X)\) such that for every integer \(x\in([X,2X]\cap\mathbb Z)\setminus \mathcal E_X\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\), with implied constants absolute; in particular, they are uniform in \(\lambda\), \(\varepsilon\), and \(\theta\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h/\log X\); equivalently, the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in \((x,x+h]\) is bounded above and below by positive constant multiples of \(h/\log X\) outside that exceptional set.

sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the authors “construct[] appropriate comparison functions” of the form
\[
 u^{\pm}(x,y,t)\coloneqq\Phi_{c^{*}}\left(\frac{y-V(x,t)}{\sqrt{1+|\nabla_{x}V|^{2}}}\mp q(t)\right)\pm p(t)\chi \left( e^{\lambda (y-c_*t)}\right),
\]
where $V$ solves
\[
V_{t}=\Delta_{x}V+\frac{c^{*}}{2}|\nabla_{x} V|^2+c_*,
\]
$\lambda<\lambda_1$, and $\chi$ is smooth with “$\chi(s)=s$ ($s \le 1/2$) and $\chi(s)=1$ ($s \ge 1$).” They then “show that $u^+$ (resp. $u^-$) becomes a supersolution (resp. a subsolution) for the problem
\begin{aligned}\label{Rea-Diff}
&u_{t}=\Delta u+f(u), && x\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}, \, y\in\mathbb{R}, \, t>0, \\
&u(x,y,0)=u_{0}(x,y), && x\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}, \, y\in\mathbb{R}.
\end{aligned}
if $p(t)$, $q(t)$ and the initial data for $V(x,t)$ are chosen appropriately.”

In establishing part (ii) of the main theorem, they “use[] these comparison functions and apply[] the fact that the solution $U$ of
\begin{equation}\label{MCF}
\left\{
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{U_t}{\sqrt{1+|\nabla_x U|^2}}=\mathrm{div}\left(\frac{\nabla_x U}{\sqrt{1+|\nabla_x U|}}\right)+c_*,&&x\in\mathbf{R}^{n-1},t>0,\\
&U(x,0)=\gamma(x,\tau_{\varepsilon}),&&x\in\mathbf{R}^{n-1},
\end{aligned}
\right.
\end{equation}
is approximated by $V$.”

mcq.question:
Let n\ge 2, let \(\Delta=\partial_{x_1}^2+\cdots+\partial_{x_{n-1}}^2+\partial_y^2\), and let \(u=u(x,y,t)\) solve
\[
u_t=\Delta u+f(u), \qquad (x,y)\in \mathbb{R}^{n-1}\times\mathbb{R},\ t>0,
\]
with initial data \(u(x,y,0)=u_0(x,y)\). Assume the nonlinearity \(f\) satisfies
\[
f(0)=f(1)=0,\quad f'(0)>0,\quad f'(1)<0,\quad f(s)>0\ \text{for }s\in(0,1),\quad f(s)<0\ \text{for }s\in(-\infty,0)\cup(1,\infty).
\]
Let \(c=c_* > 2\sqrt{f'(0)}\), where \(c_*\) is the minimal speed of the one-dimensional traveling fronts, let \(\Phi_c\) be the corresponding one-dimensional traveling-wave profile, i.e.
\[
\Phi_c''+c\Phi_c'+f(\Phi_c)=0,\qquad \lim_{z\to-\infty}\Phi_c(z)=1,\quad \lim_{z\to\infty}\Phi_c(z)=0,
\]
and let \(\lambda_+\) be the larger root of \(\lambda^2+c\lambda+f'(0)=0\). Suppose also that
\[
\liminf_{y\to-\infty}\inf_{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}} u_0(x,y)>0,
\]
and
\[
\limsup_{y\to\infty}\sup_{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}} u_0(x,y)e^{-\lambda_1 y}<\infty
\quad\text{for some }\lambda_1<\lambda_+.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the large-time asymptotic behavior of \(u\) and of the level set where \(u=\Phi_c(0)\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exist a smooth function \(\gamma=\gamma(x,t)\) and a time \(T>0\) such that for every \(t\ge T\),
\[
\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}\times\mathbb{R}: u(x,y,t)=\Phi_c(0)\}=\{(x,y): y=\gamma(x,t)\},
\]
and moreover
\[
\lim_{t\to\infty}\sup_{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^n}\left|u(x,y,t)-\Phi_c\!\left(\frac{y-\gamma(x,t)}{\sqrt{1+|\nabla_x\gamma(x,t)|^2}}\right)\right|=0.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exist a smooth function \(\gamma=\gamma(x,t)\) and a time \(T>0\) such that for every \(t\ge T\),
\[
\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}\times\mathbb{R}: u(x,y,t)=\Phi_c(0)\}=\{(x,y): y=\gamma(x,t)\}.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exist a smooth function \(\gamma=\gamma(x,t)\) and a time \(T>0\) such that for every \(t\ge T\),
\[
\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}\times\mathbb{R}: u(x,y,t)=\Phi_c(0)\}=\{(x,y): y=\gamma(x,t)\},
\]
moreover \(\gamma\) itself satisfies for all \(t\ge T\)

\]

\]

There exist a smooth function \(\gamma=\gamma(x,t)\) and a time \(T>0\) such that for every \(t\ge T\),

theorem:
\label{theorem:main}
    Let $f$ be a cuspidal modular form of weight $k$ on $\SL_2(\BZ)$. For any integers $l\geq2$ and $d\geq1$ and $dl>4$, one has
    \[\sum_{n\leq x}  \lambda^l_{\Sym^d f}(n)=xP_{d,l}(\log x)+O\left(x^{\theta_{d,l}+\epsilon}\right).\]
    Here $P_{d,l}$ is a polynomial of degree $K_{0,d,l}-1$, where $K_{i,d,l}$ denote the Kostka number defined in sections \ref{section:Weylmod} and \ref{section:Kostka number}. The exponent $\theta_{d, l}$ is given by
    $$\theta_{d,l}=1-1/\left((d+1)^l/2-4K_{0,d,l}/21-K_{1,d,l}/3-5K_{2,d,l}/14\right).$$,
sketch:
The post-theorem introduction contains a \subsection{Proof Sketch} explaining the strategy for Theorem~\ref{theorem:main}:

- **Perron's formula / contour integral reduction.** The “starting point is Perron's formula,” which converts estimating \(\sum_{n\le x}\lambda^l_{\Sym^d f}(n)\) into evaluating “a contour integral of the associated Dirichlet series” \(\sum_{n\ge1}\lambda^l_{\Sym^d f}(n)n^{-s}\).

- **Relate the Dirichlet series to an \(L\)-function via Euler product.** This Dirichlet series is “intimately related via its Euler product” to \(L((\Sym^d f)^{\otimes l},s)\), corresponding to the \(\ell\)-adic representation \(\Sym^d(\rho_f)^{\otimes l}\) (cf. Proposition~\ref{proposition:dirichletseriescomp}).

- **Decompose the tensor representation and factor the \(L\)-function.** A “key insight” is decomposing the “high-dimensional tensor representation into a direct sum of irreducible constituents,” which are “of the form \(\Sym^i\rho_f\).” Hence
  \[
  L\bigl((\Sym^d f)^{\otimes l},s\bigr)=\prod_{i=0}^{ld} L(\Sym^i f,s)^{K_{i,d,l}}.
  \]

- **Use automorphy/entireness and subconvexity to get the asymptotic.** The “automorphy and entirety of these symmetric power \(L\)-functions,” due to Newton--Thorne, are “crucial,” and the “final asymptotic formulas are then derived by combining this factorization with established subconvexity bounds for the involved \(L\)-functions.”

(An additional sketch is given for the binary quadratic form theorem \(\ref{thm:binary}\): one studies \(\sum \lambda^l_{\Sym^d f}(n)r(n,Q)n^{-s}\), expresses \(\sum r(n,Q)n^{-s}\) via \(\zeta_F(s)\) when \(h(D)=1\) or as a finite sum of Hecke \(L\)-functions in general, reducing to Rankin--Selberg type \(L\)-functions \(L((\Sym^d f)^{\otimes l}\otimes \rho_{f_\chi},s)\), and then applies subconvexity to their decomposed components.)

theorem:
\label{geometricallyFiniteNearOrbit}
    Suppose that a group $G$ admits a non-elementary proper action on a proper Gromov hyperbolic space $X$. Then the action is geometrically infinite if and only if there exists an escaping sequence of hyperbolic elements in $G$.
sketch: A proof sketch for Theorem~\ref{geometricallyFiniteNearOrbit} is indicated in the “Structure of the paper” paragraph: in Section~3, “assuming the condition in Theorem~\ref{geometricallyFiniteNearOrbit}, we construct geometric objects analogous to those in a cusp-uniform action” and then “prove that every point in the limit set $\Lambda G$ is either conical or bounded parabolic, thereby establishing the theorem.” The introduction also notes that the approach “relies only on elementary arguments in hyperbolic geometry and does not invoke the Margulis lemma,” and that a “key technical lemma (Lemma~\ref{producingHyperbolicElements}) for producing hyperbolic elements under a dynamical condition is presented” in Section~2.

mcq.question: Let \(\mathbb R_+\) denote the positive real numbers. Let \(\mathcal M\subseteq \mathbb R^n\) be a nondegenerate submanifold, and let \(\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n:\mathbb R_+\to(0,1)\) be non-increasing functions. A point \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbb R^n\) is called \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable if there exist infinitely many integers \(q\ge 1\) and integers \(p_1,\dots,p_n\) such that \(|qx_i-p_i|<\psi_i(q)\) for every \(i\). Which statement gives the correct criterion, in terms of \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots \psi_n(q)\), for whether almost all points of \(\mathcal M\) (with respect to the induced Lebesgue measure on \(\mathcal M\)) are \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable?

correct_choice.text: The set of \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable points on \(\mathcal M\) has full measure (with respect to the induced Lebesgue measure on \(\mathcal M\)) if \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots \psi_n(q)\) diverges, and has measure zero if that series converges.

choices.label: B

For $n\ge1$ and $0\le m<n$, $H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\cong\mathbb{Z}^2$

sketch:
The introduction outlines a proof of
\begin{conjecture}[Noferini--Williams \cite{MR4418964}]\label{conj}
For $n\ge1$ and $0\le m<n$, $H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\cong\mathbb{Z}^2$
if and only if 
$n\equiv0\pmod6$ and 
$m\equiv2\pmod n.$
\end{conjecture}
as follows. The forward implication is obtained by exhibiting “cyclotomic roots of order six for the trinomial $1+t^m-t$ under the stated congruences.” For the converse, the paper uses an arithmetic method: the “$\mathbb{Z}$-rank~2, torsion-free condition reduces to $\operatorname{Res}(F,G)=1$,” with
\[
F=\frac{1+t^m-t}{\Phi_6(t)},\quad
G=\frac{t^n-1}{\Phi_6(t)}.
\]
The rest of the argument is “a minimality argument.” First, we use the technical results underpinning the proof of the main theorem:
\label{tech}

Our first result extends \cite[Corol
In this part the conjecture is proved for the families $H(n, 2 \pm n/3)$ and $H(n, 2 + n/2)$, and “strong necessary conditions on $n$ and $m$ that any minimal counterexample must satisfy” are derived. theorem:
\label{prob:main}
Let $S\in \mathbb S_n$ be a degree $n$ singular surface in $\mathbb P^3$. In the closure of which Severi varieties $V^{\mathbb P^3, |\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|}_\delta$ is the point $[S]$ corresponding to $S$ contained? In particular, compute $\delta_S$.
sketch:
The post-theorem text does not give a proof of Problem~\ref{prob:main} (it is posed as a question), but it does outline the deformation-theoretic method used to obtain answers in the two study cases.

Key steps of the deformation argument (cases (a) and (b)):
\begin{itemize}
\item Work with \emph{regular} components of $V^{\mathbb P^3,|\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|}_\delta$, i.e. those for which for a general $[S]\in V$ one has $h^1(\mathbb P^3,\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)\otimes\mathcal I_N)=0$ (the $\delta$ singular points give independent conditions). Note: if $[S]$ is in the closure of a regular component for $\delta$, then it is also in the closure for any $\delta'\le \delta$ since “the nodes in a regular component ... can be independently smoothed.”
\item Construct a flat family $\mathcal X\to\mathbb D$ such that for $t\neq 0$, $\mathcal X_t\cong\mathbb P^3$, and the central fiber is a normal-crossing union $\mathcal X_0=A\cup\Theta$ of two smooth components meeting transversally along a surface $E$.
\item Let $f:S'\to S$ be the minimal resolution of the starting surface, with a curve $C\subset S'$ mapped to the singular locus of $S$. Realize $S'$ as a surface $S_A\subset A$ with $S_A\cap E=C$.
\item Construct a surface $S_\Theta\subset\Theta$ with $S_\Theta\cap E=C$ so that $S_0=S_A\cup S_\Theta$ is a Cartier divisor on $\mathcal X_0$.
\item Arrange that $S_\Theta$ has $\delta$ nodes off $E$ “that impose independent conditions to the surfaces in the linear system $|S_0|$ on $\mathcal X_0$.”
\item Apply \cite[Thm.~4.6]{CG}: if those $\delta$ nodes impose independent conditions, then $S_0$ deforms to a surface $S_t\subset\mathcal X_t\cong\mathbb P^3$ of degree $n$ with $\delta$ nodes giving independent conditions. Conclude that $S$ lies in the closure of a \emph{regular} component of $V^{\mathbb P^3,|\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|}_\delta$.
\end{itemize}

For case (b), the outline adds that one “rel[ies] on the existence of suitable rational surfaces of degree $n$ with a line $r$ of points of multiplicity $n-2$ and with $3n-4-\epsilon$ nodes off $r$, that impose independent conditions ...,” which is established later.

For $\delta\ge 1$, let $V^{\mathbb P^3,|\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|}_\delta\subset |\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|$ be the Severi variety of surfaces of degree $n$ with exactly $\delta$ ordinary double points (nodes) and no other singularities. What is to be determined about $[S]$ in relation to the Severi varieties $V^{\mathbb P^3,|\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|}_\delta$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: One must determine for which values of $\delta$ the point $[S]$ lies in the closure of a regular component of $V^{\mathbb P^3,|\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|}_\delta$, and in particular compute the largest such value.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One must determine at least one value of $\delta$ for which the point $[S]$ lies in the closure of $V^{\mathbb P^3,|\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^3}(n)|}_\delta$.

theorem:
\label{maintheo}Let $(M, g)$ be a Lorentzian manifold of dimension at least $3$ and let $\bf P$ be its pseudo-group of local conformal transformations, that is the collection 
 of all conformal diffeomorphisms between open subsets of $M$. We assume $M$ locally conformally homogeneous, i.e.~$M$ is an orbit of $\bf P$. Then one of the following possibilities holds:

 \begin{enumerate}

 \item $\bf P$ preserves a Lorentzian metric in the $g$-conformal class.

 \item $(M, g)$ is conformally flat. 

 \item $(M, g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to a plane wave that is isometrically locally homogeneous.  More precisely, there exists a homogeneous plane wave $X$, such that 
 $(M, [g])$ is modeled on $(\Conf(X), X)$.
 \end{enumerate}
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the argument is described as follows.

- Define the weakly essential case: when the pseudo-group $\mathbf{P}$ does not preserve any metric in the $g$-conformal class.

- As in \cite{AlekseevskyGalaev25}, start by using weak essentiality to get “the existence of an element in the isotropy group that is not contained in $\mathbf{SO}(T_pM)$.” The earlier approach relied on connectedness of the isotropy group to “provide a specific element in its Lie algebra,” but “in our setting … we cannot directly conclude the existence of such an element.”

- Use a different approach: “work only with the local isotropy of local conformal transformations,” and apply “powerful tools from Gromov's theory of rigid transformations … and from algebraic groups,” which “allow us to reduce the problem to the Lie algebra level … and in particular to obtain a hyperbolic element in the isotropy algebra that is not in $\mathfrak{so}(T_pM)$.”

- Because only local homogeneity is assumed, “pass to the universal cover, where weak essentiality is preserved.” On a “simply connected real-analytic manifold, local conformal vector fields extend to global ones,” so one can “work with the Lie algebra of conformal vector fields on the universal cover.”

- Then “use the decomposition of the Lie algebra of conformal vector fields induced by the hyperbolic element to derive the existence of a codimension-one lightlike foliation of \textit{Heisenberg type}, i.e., with a transitive action of a Heisenberg algebra.”

- Finally, combine this with “the new characterization of plane waves obtained later,” which “leads to the generalization in the main theorem.”

mcq.question: Let $(M,g)$ be a Lorentzian manifold of dimension at least $3$. Assume that $M$ is locally conformally homogeneous, meaning that its local conformal pseudogroup (the collection of conformal diffeomorphisms between open subsets of $M$) acts transitively on $M$. Which description gives the complete case-by-case classification of such manifolds?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to an isometrically locally homogeneous plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ in the sense that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Einstein Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to an isometrically locally homogeneous plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ in the sense that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to a plane wave, in the sense that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of some plane wave.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or the universal cover of $M$ is locally conformally equivalent to an isometrically locally homogeneous plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ in the sense that every point of the universal cover has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Every such $(M,g)$ satisfies at least one of these three alternatives: the local conformal pseudogroup preserves some Lorentzian metric in the conformal class $[g]$ of $g$; or $(M,g)$ is conformally flat; or $(M,g)$ is locally conformally equivalent to a locally symmetric plane wave, equivalently to a homogeneous plane wave $X$ with parallel curvature tensor such that every point of $M$ has a neighborhood conformally diffeomorphic to an open subset of $X$.

sketch_usage_meta.tampered_component: preserved metric strengthened to Einstein

sketch_usage_meta.label: C
sketch_usage_meta.tampered_component: dropped the isometrically locally homogeneous/homogeneous-plane-wave refinement in the third alternative

theorem:
\label{main}
Let $H$ be a split classical group over $F$, 
and let $\phi \in \Phi_\mathrm{temp}(H(F))$. 
Suppose that one of the following holds: 
\begin{description}
\item[(B)]
$H = \SO_{2n+1}$, $p > 6n+3$ and $\phi$ is arbitrary; 
\item[(C)]
$H = \Sp_{2n}$, $p > 6n$ and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$; 
\item[(D)]
$H = \SO_{2n}$, $p > 6n$ and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$. 
\end{description}
Set $\psi = \widehat\phi$. 

\begin{enumerate}
\item
There exists $\pi \in \Pi_\psi$ such that 
\[
d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi) \in \bar\mathcal{N}(\pi)^{\max}.
\]
\item
For $\pi \in \Pi_\psi$ and for $\oo_H^\mathrm{st} \in \bar\mathcal{N}(\pi)$, 
if $\oo_H^\mathrm{st} \not= d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$, then 
\[
\dim(\oo_H^\mathrm{st}) < \dim(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)).
\]
Here, $\dim(\oo_H^\mathrm{st})$ is the Zariski dimension of the orbit as an algebraic variety.
\item
If we further assume the following hypothesis, then the corresponding upper-bound conjecture holds for $\phi$, i.e., 
\[
\bar\mathcal{N}(\pi)^{\max} \leq d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)
\]
for any $\pi \in \Pi_\psi$.

\begin{hyp}\label{hypo}
Let $\oo_H^\st \in \NN(\h(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Define $\oo_G^\st \in \NN(\g^\theta(\overline{F}))$ by 
\[
\oo_G^\st = d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee)d_{H}(\oo_H^\st)). 
\]
Then for any constants $\{c_{\oo_H} \;|\; \oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st\}$ such that 
$\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H}$ is stable, 
there exist constants $\{a_{\oo_G} \;|\; \oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st\}$ such that 
\[
\sum_{\oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st} a_{\oo_G} \hat\mu_{\oo_G}
\quad\text{is equal to the transfer of}\quad
\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H} 
\]
on a small enough neighborhood of $\theta \in G(F) \rtimes \theta$.
\end{hyp}

\begin{conj}\label{upper}
Let $\phi \in \Phi(H(F))$. 
Then for any $\pi \in \Pi_\phi$, we have
\[
\bar\NN(\hat\pi)^{\max} \leq d_{H^\vee}(\Ad(H^\vee)N_\phi).
\]
Moreover, if $\phi$ is tempered, 
then the equality holds for some $\pi \in \Pi_\phi$. 
\end{conj}
\end{enumerate}
sketch:
The proof of Theorem~\ref{main} follows Konno~\cite{K} and Varma~\cite{V}: the "main idea is to compare local character expansions via \emph{endoscopic character identities}." Fix $\phi\in\Phi_\mathrm{temp}(H(F))$, set $\psi=\widehat\phi$, and embed into $G=\GL_m$ via the standard map $H^\vee\to G^\vee$. This yields a self-dual $\pi_\psi$ of $G(F)$, extended to $\tilde\pi_\psi$ on $G\rtimes\langle\theta\rangle$, whose twisted character $\Theta_{\tilde\pi_\psi}$ matches Arthur’s stable distribution
\[
S\Theta_{\psi}=\sum_{\pi\in\Pi_\psi}\langle s_\psi,\pi\rangle_\psi\,\Theta_\pi
\]
by endoscopic character identities (Arthur’s (T1)), and similarly for $S\Theta_{\psi,s}$ (Arthur’s (T2)).

(1) "The first step" is an analogue in the twisted $\GL_m$ case: using Clozel’s twisted local character expansion
\[
\Theta_{\tilde\pi_\psi}=\sum_{\oo_G\in\mathcal N(\mathfrak g^\theta(F))} c_{\oo_G}(\tilde\pi_\psi)\,\hat\mu_{\oo_G},
\]
one defines $\bar\mathcal N_\theta(\tilde\pi_\psi)^{\max}$ and proves the following theorem.
\begin{thm}\label{twist}
Let $\phi \colon W_F \times \SL_2(\C) \rightarrow G^\vee$ 
be a self-dual tempered $L$-parameter for $G(F) = \GL_m(F)$, 
and let $\pi_\psi$ be the Zelevinsky dual of the irreducible tempered representation 
$\pi_\phi$ corresponding to $\phi$. 
Then 
\[
\bar\NN_\theta(\tl\pi_\psi)^{\max} = \{d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee) N_\phi)\}.
\]
Moreover, $c_{\oo_G}(\tl\pi_\psi) = c_{\oo_G'}(\tl\pi_\psi)$ 
for any rational orbits $\oo_G$ and $\oo_G'$ contained in $d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee) N_\phi)$.
\end{thm}
This uses results of Konno, Varma, and M\oe glin--Waldspurger.

(2) Setting $\oo_H^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$ and $\oo_G^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{G^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(G^\vee)N_\phi)$, "the second step" is the following transfer statement.
\begin{thm}\label{transfer}
Let $\oo_H^\st \in \NN(\h(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Define $\oo_G^\st \in \NN(\g^\theta(\overline{F}))$ by 
\[
\oo_G^\st = d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee)d_{H}(\oo_H^\st)). 
\]
Then for each rational orbit $\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st$, 
there exists a constant $\gamma_{\oo_H} \in \C$ such that 
\[
\sum_{\oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st} \hat\mu_{\oo_G}
\quad\text{is equal to the transfer of }\quad
\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} \gamma_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H}
\]
on a small enough neighborhood of $\theta \in G(F) \rtimes \theta$.
\end{thm}
The following hypothesis is described as an analogue of this transfer result.
\begin{hyp}\label{hypo}
Let $\oo_H^\st \in \NN(\h(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Define $\oo_G^\st \in \NN(\g^\theta(\overline{F}))$ by 
\[
\oo_G^\st = d_{G^\vee}(\Ad(G^\vee)d_{H}(\oo_H^\st)). 
\]
Then for any constants $\{c_{\oo_H} \;|\; \oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st\}$ such that 
$\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H}$ is stable, 
there exist constants $\{a_{\oo_G} \;|\; \oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st\}$ such that 
\[
\sum_{\oo_G \subset \oo_G^\st} a_{\oo_G} \hat\mu_{\oo_G}
\quad\text{is equal to the transfer of}\quad
\sum_{\oo_H \subset \oo_H^\st} c_{\oo_H} \hat\mu_{\oo_H} 
\]
on a small enough neighborhood of $\theta \in G(F) \rtimes \theta$.
\end{hyp}

(3) "The third step" relates Waldspurger’s explicit map for standard endoscopy,
\[
\oo_H^{\mathrm{st}}=W(\oo_{H_1}^{\mathrm{st}},\oo_{H_2}^{\mathrm{st}}),
\]
to Spaltenstein duality using the proposition below.
\begin{prop}\label{WS}
For $i \in \{1,2\}$, let $\oo_{H_i}^\st \in \NN(\h_i(\overline{F}))$ be a special nilpotent orbit.
Then 
\[
W(\oo_{H_1}^\st, \oo_{H_2}^\st) 
\leq d_{H^\vee}(\Ad(H^\vee)\xi(d_{H_1}(\oo_{H_1}^\st), d_{H_2}(\oo_{H_2}^\st))). 
\]
\end{prop}

Finally, "combining the two transfer results," one relates the local character expansions of $S\Theta_\psi$ and $S\Theta_{\psi,s}$ to those of $\Theta_{\tilde\pi_\psi}$ and $S\Theta_{\psi_1}\otimes S\Theta_{\psi_2}$, and then "by induction on $m$" establishes the main theorem (later in the paper). The condition $(\star)$ in Cases (C) and (D) is needed to apply Waldspurger’s theorem, equivalently ensuring the relevant endoscopic groups $H_1\times H_2$ are unramified.

mcq.question: Let $H$ be a split classical group over a nonarchimedean local field $F$ with Lie algebra $\mathfrak h$, and let $\phi\in\Phi_{\mathrm{temp}}(H(F))$ be a tempered $L$-parameter. Assume one of the following: (i) $H=\mathrm{SO}_{2n+1}$, $p>6n+3$, and $\phi$ is arbitrary; (ii) $H=\mathrm{Sp}_{2n}$, $p>6n$, and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$; or (iii) $H=\mathrm{SO}_{2n}$, $p>6n$, and $\phi$ satisfies $(\star)$. Set $\psi=\widehat\phi$, and let $\Pi_\psi$ be the $L$-packet attached to $\psi$. If $d\phi|_{\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)}:\mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb C)\to \mathfrak h^\vee$ is the derivative of $\phi|_{\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)}$, let $N_\phi\in\mathfrak h^\vee$ be the image of $\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$, and let $d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$ be the stable nilpotent orbit in $\mathfrak h(\overline F)$ obtained from the adjoint orbit of $N_\phi$ in the dual group $H^\vee$ via the Spaltenstein duality map $d_{H^\vee}$. For $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$, let $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ denote the set of stable nilpotent orbits in $\mathfrak h(\overline F)$ attached to $\pi$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the dimensions of the orbits in $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ under these hypotheses?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$, there exists some $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ such that
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})=\dim\bigl(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)\bigr).
\]
In other words, each member of the packet has a stable nilpotent orbit of the same dimension as $d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$ such that for every $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$, the equality
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})=\dim\bigl(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)\bigr)
\]
can occur only when $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There is a stable nilpotent orbit $\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}$, independent of $\pi$, such that for every $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$ and every $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ one has
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})\leq \dim(\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}),
\]
with equality only when $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}=\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}$; moreover one may take $\mathcal O_0^{\mathrm{st}}=d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$, every orbit $\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}}\in\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)^{\max}$ satisfies
\[
\dim(\mathcal O_H^{\mathrm{st}})=\dim\bigl(d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)\bigr),
\]
so the maximal dimension occurring in $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ is always the dimension of $d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

theorem:
\label{thm:MinimalSurfaceExistence}
For every $p, q \geq 1$, there exists a minimal embedding of $\mathbb{S}^p \times \mathbb{S}^q \times \mathbb{S}^1$ in $\mathbb{S}^{p+q+2}$.
sketch: Theorem~\ref{thm:MinimalSurfaceExistence} is described as being “obtained from the study of capillary minimal cones as the other endpoint of a capillary interpolation between free-boundary minimal surfaces and the one-phase problem.” The introduction explains the proof strategy via a capillary-to-free-boundary limiting argument: using “a bi-orthogonal symmetry, the capillary minimal surface equation reduces to a free boundary ODE.” For capillary angles $\theta\in(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2})$, “shooting techniques that match the contact angle are well-defined,” and the authors “show that the resulting solutions converge uniformly to an infinite-slope shot.” Since doubling across the totally geodesic boundary of $\mathbb{S}^n_+$ turns a free-boundary surface into an embedded minimal surface in the sphere, this limiting “infinite-slope shot” is used in establishing the main theorem.

mcq.question: Let \(\mathbb S^n\) denote the \(n\)-dimensional sphere. For integers \(p,q\ge 1\), call an embedding \(f:M\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^N\) minimal if its image is a minimal submanifold of \(\mathbb S^N\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of minimal embeddings of \(\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\) under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+1}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists some integer \(N=N(p,q)\) and a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{N}\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\) whose image is contained in some totally geodesic equatorial \(\mathbb S^{p+q+1}\subset \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\) obtained by reflecting across an equatorial \(\mathbb S^{p+q+1}\) a smooth minimal hypersurface that meets that equator at a constant angle \(\theta\in(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2})\).

theorem:
\label{thme: codimension-one-homology}
        Let $X$ be a metric space with finite Hausdorff $n$-measure that is homeomorphic to a closed, oriented, connected smooth $n$-manifold. Suppose that $X$ has a metric fundamental class. Then, there exists a surjective homomorphism from $H_{n-1}^\textup{IC}(X)$ to $H_{n-1}(X)$.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the argument proceeds as follows.

1. **Start from the smooth/Riemannian case.** For a closed, oriented, connected Riemannian $n$-manifold $M$ and a primitive class $[\eta]\in H_{n-1}(M)$, there exists a smooth map $f\colon M\to \mathbb{S}^1$ such that for each regular value $p\in \mathbb{S}^1$, the preimage $N_p=f^{-1}(p)$ is a smooth submanifold representing $[\eta]$. Using that $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(M)$ and $H_{n-1}(M)$ are isomorphic, one gets an analogous statement for elements in $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(M)$.

2. **Pass to the metric space via a Lipschitz approximation.** Let $X$ be homeomorphic to $M$ and assume $X$ has a metric fundamental class $T\in \mathbf{I}_n(X)$. Approximate the homeomorphism $\varrho\colon X\to M$ by a Lipschitz map $\varphi\colon X\to M$, and consider the slices $\langle T, f\circ\varphi, p\rangle$ and $\langle\llbracket M\rrbracket,f,p\rangle$.

3. **Identify slices on $M$ and relate them via pushforward.** For a.e. $p\in \mathbb{S}^1$, the slice $\langle\llbracket M\rrbracket,f,p\rangle$ equals $\llbracket N_p\rrbracket$. Since pushforward and slicing commute and $T$ is a metric fundamental class,
\[
\varphi_\#\langle T,f \circ \varphi, p\rangle = \langle\varphi_\#T,f,p\rangle = \langle \llbracket M\rrbracket,f,p\rangle = \llbracket N_p\rrbracket
\]
for a.e. $p\in \mathbb{S}^1$.

4. **Produce preimages of generators and get surjectivity to $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(M)$.** Thus for a.e. $p$, the slice $\langle T,f\circ\varphi,p\rangle$ defines a class $[\xi]\in H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(X)$ that is sent to $[\eta]$ by the map $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(X)\to H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(M)$ induced by $\varphi$. Repeating this “for each generator of $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(M)$” yields a surjective homomorphism $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(X)\to H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(M)$.

5. **Conclude the desired surjection to $H_{n-1}(X)$.** Finally, use that $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(M)\cong H_{n-1}(M)$ and $H_{n-1}(M)\cong H_{n-1}(X)$ to obtain the stated surjective homomorphism from $H_{n-1}^{\textup{IC}}(X)$ to $H_{n-1}(X).$

mcq.question: Let \(X\) be a metric space with finite Hausdorff \(n\)-measure, and assume that \(X\) is homeomorphic to a closed, oriented, connected smooth \(n\)-manifold. Suppose moreover that \(X\) has a metric fundamental class, meaning that for some closed, oriented, connected Riemannian \(n\)-manifold \(M\) homeomorphic to \(X\), there exists an integral current \(T\in \mathbf{I}_n(X)\) with \(\partial T=0\), \(\|T\|\le C\,\mathcal H^n\) for some \(C>0\), and \(\varphi_{\#}T=\deg(\varphi)\,\llbracket M\rrbracket\) for every Lipschitz map \(\varphi:X\to M\). Here \(H_{n-1}^{\mathrm{IC}}(X)\) denotes the \((n-1)\)-st homology group coming from integral currents on \(X\), and \(H_{n-1}(X)\) denotes the usual \((n-1)\)-st singular homology group of \(X\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a homomorphism relating \(H_{n-1}^{\mathrm{IC}}(X)\) and \(H_{n-1}(X)\) under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists an isomorphism from \(H_{n-1}^{\mathrm{IC}}(X)\) to \(H_{n-1}(X)\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a surjective homomorphism from \(H_{n-1}^{\mathrm{IC}}(X)\otimes \mathbb{Q}\) to \(H_{n-1}(X)\otimes \mathbb{Q}\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every closed, oriented, connected Riemannian \(n\)-manifold \(M\) homeomorphic to \(X\) and every Lipschitz map \(\varphi:X\to M\) of nonzero degree, the induced homomorphism \(\varphi_*:H_{n-1}^{\mathrm{IC}}(X)\to H_{n-1}^{\mathrm{IC}}(M)\) is surjective.

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a surjective homomorphism from \(H_n^{\mathrm{IC}}(X)\) to \(H_{n-1}(X)\).

theorem:
\label{thm1.1}
	Assume $N\ge1$, $m\ge2$, and \textbf{\textup{$(A_1)$--$(A_3)$}}. Then for $\varepsilon>0$, 
	\begin{equation}\label{eq1.1}
	\begin{cases}
		-\Delta u_i + u_i
		= \mu_i Q_\varepsilon(x-y_i) |u_i|^{2p-2}u_i
		+ \sum_{j\neq i} \lambda_{ij} |u_j|^{p} |u_i|^{p-2}u_i, \\[6pt]
		u_i \in H^1(\mathbb{R}^N),\ i=1,\dots,m.
	\end{cases}	
\end{equation}
	admits a sequence of sign-changing solutions
	\[
	\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon=(u^{(k)}_{\varepsilon,1},\dots,u^{(k)}_{\varepsilon,m})\in H, ~ k\in\mathbb{N}^*,
	\]
	satisfying
	\[
	0<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
	< \cdots < J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
    < J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k+1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
	< \cdots, ~\text{and}~ \lim\limits_{k\to\infty}
	J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)=+\infty.
	\]

mcq.question:
Let \(N\ge 1\), \(m\ge 2\), and assume the following: \(1<p<\frac{2^*}{2}\) if \(N\ge 3\), and \(p>1\) if \(N=1,2\), where \(2^*=\frac{2N}{N-2}\) for \(N\ge 3\); \(\mu_i>0\) for \(i=1,\dots,m\); \(\lambda_{ij}=\lambda_{ji}<0\) for all \(i\ne j\); and \(Q_\varepsilon(x-y_i):=Q(\varepsilon x-y_i)\), where \(Q\in C(\mathbb{R}^N)\) is nonnegative, has bounded support, and there exist exactly pairwise distinct points \(y_1,\dots,y_m\in\mathbb{R}^N\) such that \(Q(-y_i)=\|Q\|_\infty\) for all \(i=1,\dots,m\). For \(\varepsilon>0\), consider the coupled elliptic system
\[
\begin{cases}
-\Delta u_i + u_i
= \mu_i Q_\varepsilon(x-y_i) |u_i|^{2p-2}u_i
+ \displaystyle\sum_{j\neq i} \lambda_{ij} |u_j|^{p} |u_i|^{p-2}u_i, \\[4pt]
u_i \in H^1(\mathbb{R}^N),\quad i=1,\dots,m,
\end{cases}
\]
and write \(\mathbf u=(u_1,\dots,u_m)\in H\) with \(H=(H^1(\mathbb R^N))^m\). Let \(J_\varepsilon\) denote the associated energy functional. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of sign-changing solutions for this system under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits a sequence of sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon=(u^{(k)}_{\varepsilon,1},\dots,u^{(k)}_{\varepsilon,m})\in H\), indexed by positive integers \(k\), such that
\[
0<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
<\cdots< J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
< J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k+1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
<\cdots,
\]
and
\[
\lim_{k\to\infty} J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)=+\infty.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(0<\varepsilon<\varepsilon_0\), the system admits a sequence of sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\in H\) with
\[
0<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)<\cdots<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k+1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)<\cdots,
\]
and
\[
\lim_{k\to\infty}J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)=+\infty,
\]
but this multiplicity conclusion is available only for sufficiently small \(\varepsilon\), not for every fixed \(\varepsilon>0\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits infinitely many distinct sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf u\in H\).

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits infinitely many sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\in H\), but their energy levels remain bounded above for that fixed \(\varepsilon\); equivalently, there exists \(C_\varepsilon<\infty\) such that
\[
J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)\le C_\varepsilon\qquad\text{for all }k.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits a least-energy sign-changing solution \(\mathbf u_\varepsilon\in H\), and up to the natural sign symmetry \(\mathbf u\mapsto-\mathbf u\), this is the only sign-changing solution guaranteed by the theory.

theorem:
Assume that (F1-5) hold true. 
Then there exists a nontrivial periodic solution for~\begin{align}\label{eq:NDE-spatial}\tag{*}
    -i\gamma^0 \gamma^k\p_k \psi + m\gamma^0\psi -a\psi-\nabla F(\psi)=0.
\end{align}, which is in~$C^1$.
sketch: We outline the proof here. The equation~\eqref{eq:NDE-spatial} admits a variational structure, namely it is the Euler--Lagrange equation of an action functional~$J$ (see Section~\ref{sect:variational str}), but $J$ doesn't satisfy the Palais-Smale condition. As a remedy we introduce a coercive perturbation multiplied by a small~$\varepsilon\in (0,1]$ (Section~\ref{sect:perturbation}) and show the perturbed functional meets the Palais--Smale condition, although the $(PS)_c$ sequences have a bound depending on~$\frac{1}{\varepsilon}$. Using spectral information of the Dirac operator, the perturbed functionals admit a linking structure in levels with estimates uniform in~$\varepsilon$; although the linking sets are infinite dimensional, one uses the negative gradient flow together with the Leray--Schauder degree to find a nontrivial linking, yielding min-max solutions for the perturbed equations (end of Section~\ref{sect:perturbation}). Then, using the special structure of~$F$ and the perturbation term, a contradiction argument gives uniform estimates for the perturbed solutions (Section~\ref{sect:uniform estimate}); here the triviality of the kernel of the geometric Dirac operator on the three-sphere is needed. Finally, one passes along a subsequence to a nontrivial limit which is the desired nontrivial periodic solution (Section~\ref{sect:last}).

theorem:
\label{thm_A}
Let $A$ be a Noetherian domain containing a field of characteristic zero. Let $X\subset\mathbb{P}^n_A$ be a closed subscheme that is smooth over $\operatorname{Spec} A$, and $X_t$ its $t$-th thickening, as defined above. Fix an integer~$j$, and set $\mathcal{F}\colonequals\calO_{\mathbb{P}^n_A}(j)$. Then there exists a nonzero element $a\in A$ such that 
\[
\operatorname{H}^i\big(X_t,\, \calO_{X_t}\otimes_{\calO_{\mathbb{P}^n_A}}\mathcal{F}\big)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is a flat $A_a$-module for each integer $i\ge 0$, for all thickenings $t\ge 1$.
sketch:
The introduction explains that the proof of the main theorem relies on understanding the asymptotic behavior of the cohomology of powers of the ideal sheaf. The approach proceeds as follows:

1. Develop a relative theory of ampleness: the authors use the notion of relatively ample vector bundles and develop the necessary theory of $f$-ample vector bundles and establish a fiberwise criterion for ampleness for a projective morphism $f\colon X\subset\PP_S^n\to S$. The primary example is the normal bundle $\scrN_X$ for a smooth closed immersion $X\hookrightarrow\PP_S^n$.

2. Prove a uniform vanishing theorem: a key technical tool is the following uniform vanishing result for the cohomology of the symmetric powers of the conormal bundle, extending classical results of Hartshorne.

\begin{theorem}
\label{thm_uniform_vanish}
Let $S$ be a Noetherian scheme over the field of rational numbers, and $f\colon X\subset\PP_S^n\to S$ be a smooth morphism of relative dimension $d\ge 0$. Let $\calF$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$ that is flat over $S$, and set $\calF_s\colonequals \calF\otimes_{\calO_S}\kappa(s)$ for $s\in S$. Then there exists an integer $t_0>0$ such that 
\[
\HH^i\left(X_s,\, \Sym^t\big(\scrI_{X_s}/\scrI_{X_s}^2\big)\otimes_{\calO_{X_s}}\calF_s\right)\ =\ 0
\]
for all $s\in S$, $t\ge t_0$, and $i\neq d$.
\end{theorem}

3. Combine vanishing with generic freeness and local duality:

(a) Use the generic freeness of local cohomology from \cite[Theorem~B]{CRS}: the local cohomology modules $\HH_I^i(R)$ are generically free over the base ring $A$, where $I$ is an ideal of $R\colonequals A[x_0,\dots,x_n]$.

(b) Using a graded local duality argument, obtain a stabilization result for the graded components of the modules $\Ext_R^i(R/I^t,\, R)$, corresponding to thickenings.

(c) Deduce the desired flatness for cohomology of thickenings by using the identification
\[
\dlim_t\, \Ext_R^i\big(R/I^t,\, R\big)\ =\ \HH_I^i(R),
\]
and the aforementioned stabilization result for Ext modules. Concretely, one uses the following stabilization-by-injectivity statement.

\begin{proposition}
\label{prop_inject}
Let $I \subset R$ be a homogeneous ideal and $X=V(I)\subset\PP_A^n$ be the closed subscheme defined by $I$. Fix an integer $j\in\ZZ$. Assume that: 

\begin{enumerate}[\quad\rm(a)]
\item The natural projection $X\subset\PP_A^n\to\Spec A$ is a smooth morphism of relative dimension $d\ge 0$,
\item $I^t/I^{t+1}$ is $A$-flat for each $t\ge 0$,
\item $\HH_I^i(R)$ is $A$-flat for each $i\ge 0$.
\end{enumerate}
Then there is an integer $t_0>0$, such that the natural map 
\[
\CD
\psi_{B,t}^i\colon {\Ext_{R_B}^i\left(R_B/I^tR_B,\, R_B\right)}_{-j-n-1} @>>> {\Ext_{R_B}^i\left(R_B/I^{t+1}R_B,\, R_B\right)}_{-j-n-1}
\endCD
\]
is injective for each $t\ge t_0$ and $i\ge 0$, where $B$ is an arbitrary $A$-algebra, and $R_B\colonequals R\otimes_A B$.
\end{proposition}

In addition, one applies the following valuative criterion for flatness of the relevant cohomology groups.

\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion for the flatness of cohomology]
\label{prop_valuative}
Let $R= A[x_0,\dots,x_n]$ be a standard graded polynomial ring over a Noetherian domain $A$, and $I\subset R$ a homogeneous ideal. Fix $j \in \ZZ$ and $t\ge 1$. Assume that:
\begin{enumerate}[\quad\rm(a)]
\item For each $A$-algebra $V$ that is a discrete valuation ring, and for each $i\ge 0$, the natural map 
\[
\CD
{\Ext_{R_V}^i\left(R_V/I^tR_V,\, R_V\right)}_{-j-n-1} @>>> {\HH_I^i(R_V)}_{-j-n-1}
\endCD
\]
is injective, where $R_V\colonequals R\otimes_A V$,
\item $R/I^t$ is $A$-flat,
\item $\HH_I^i(R)$ is $A$-flat for each $i\ge 0$.
\end{enumerate}
Then ${\HH_\frakM^i(R/I^t)}_j$ is $A$-flat for each $i \ge 0$.
\end{lemma}

theorem:
\label{thm:chordarc}
    Let $3\leq n\leq 5$, \(\Sigma^n \subset \mathbb{R}^{n+1}\) be a two-sided, embedded minimal disk with \(\sup_{\Sigma}|A| < \infty\).
    Then there exists a constant \(c = c\bigl(n,\sup_{\Sigma}|A|\bigr) > 0\) with the following property: for every \(x\in \Sigma\) and every \(R>1\) such that the intrinsic ball \(\mathcal{B}_{R}(x)\subset \Sigma\), the connected component \(\Sigma_{x, R}\) of the Euclidean ball \(B_{R}(x)\cap \Sigma\) containing \(x\) satisfies
    \[
        \Sigma_{x, R}\subset \mathcal{B}_{cR^n}(x).
    \]
    In particular, intrinsic distances are quantitatively controlled by extrinsic distances on \(\Sigma_{x, R}\), with constants depending only on the dimension and the curvature bound.

theorem:
[Gradient regularity in Morrey spaces]\label{MainThm}
Let $u \in W^{1,p}(\Omega)$ be a local minimizer of the functional $\mathcal{F}(\cdot;\Omega)$ defined in
\begin{equation}\label{MainF}
\mathcal{F}(u;E) := \int_{\Omega} \sigma_E(x)\, F(\nabla u)\, dx,
\end{equation}
and assume that $\partial E$ is a hypersurface of class $C^1$.
Suppose that assumptions \emph{(H1)}--\emph{(H2)} are satisfied where $2<p<\frac{2n}{n-2}$.
Then, for every $0 \leq \lambda < n$,
\[
\nabla u \in L^{{p},\lambda}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega).
\]
Moreover, for every $\Omega' \Subset \Omega$, there exists a constant
$C = C\big(n, p, \nu, L, \beta, E, \operatorname{diam}(\Omega),
\operatorname{dist}(\Omega', \partial\Omega)\big) > 0$
such that
\[
\|\nabla u\|_{L^{{p},\lambda}(\Omega')} \leq C.
\]
sketch:
The proof of the main theorem is obtained as a consequence of the decay estimate for the energy stated in the following proposition.

\begin{Prop}\label{FJNL}
Let $u$ be a local minimizer of the functional $\mathcal{F}(\cdot;\Omega)$ defined
in \eqref{MainF} under the assumption of Theorem~\ref{MainThm}.
Then there exists a constant $0 < \tau_0 < 1$ such that the following holds:
for every $\tau \in (0,\tau_0)$ there exists $\varepsilon_0 = \varepsilon_0(\tau) > 0$
with the property that, if $B_r(x_0) \Subset \Omega$ and one of the following
conditions is satisfied,
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $|E \cap B_r(x_0)| < \varepsilon_0 |B_r|$,
\item $|B_r(x_0) \setminus E| < \varepsilon_0 |B_r|$,
\item there exists a half-space $H$ such that
\[
\frac{|(E \Delta H) \cap B_r(x_0)|}{|B_r|} < \varepsilon_0,
\]
\end{enumerate}
then, for every $0 < \delta < n$, the estimate
\[
\int_{B_{\tau r}(x_0)} |\nabla u|^p \, dx
\leq
C_0 \tau^{n-\delta}
\left(
\int_{B_r(x_0)} |\nabla u|^p \, dx + r^n
\right)
\]
holds, where the constant $C_0$ depends only on
$n, \nu, L, \alpha,\beta, \delta$ and $\|\nabla u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}$.
\end{Prop}

An outline is given for the proof of Proposition~\ref{FJNL}:
\begin{itemize}
\item The argument \emph{relies on decay estimates for the gradient near the interface}, first established in a \emph{flat configuration} (interface coincides with a hyperplane), treated next.
\item Starting point: a \emph{local boundedness estimate for the tangential gradient} $\nabla' u$ (derivatives parallel to the interface). This is proved in Proposition~3.2 via a \emph{difference-quotient argument} with test functions involving only tangential increments.
\item Key ingredient: a \emph{Moser iteration} applied to $Z:=|\Delta_{i,h}u|^{m/p}$. In the estimates, the full gradient $\nabla u$ appears through (H1)--(H2) and is \emph{treated as a weight}; this is cited as the origin of the restriction $2<p<\frac{2n}{n-2}$.
\item Since the Proposition~3.2 bound has a non-optimal decay exponent and constants that may depend on $\mu$, one obtains the \emph{scale-invariant Lipschitz estimate for $\nabla' u$} (Proposition~3.4) by a \emph{rescaling argument} requiring \emph{uniformity with respect to $\mu$}. This uniformity is proved in Lemma~3.3, using boundedness of $\nabla' u$ and a \emph{Poincar\'e-type inequality} for $Z:=|\partial_i u|^{m/p}$, $i=1,\dots,n-1$, which is admissible by Theorem~3.1.
\item With the tangential Lipschitz bound, the \emph{Morrey estimate in the flat case} is proved in Theorem~3.5 by a \emph{comparison argument}.
\item The \emph{general case} then follows by a \emph{standard flattening/approximation procedure} using the $C^1$ regularity of the interface, along the lines of the quadratic theory.
\end{itemize}

Additionally, Remark~\ref{Reg} notes that for proving the main theorem it would be sufficient to have Proposition~\ref{FJNL} only in case (i), though cases (ii)--(iii) are included for completeness.

theorem:
\label{thm:intro-cap-comp} 
Let $\Omega \subset \subset \mathbb{C}^n$ be a strictly pseudoconvex domain. 
\begin{itemize}
\item[(a)] There exists a constant $A>0$ such that for every Borel set $E\subset \Omega$,
$$
	{\tt c}(E) \leq A [cap(E,\Omega)]^\frac{1}{n}.
$$
\item[(b)]
Assume $D \subset \subset \Omega$ be a subdomain. There exists a constant $A'$ such that for every Borel set $E\subset D$,
$$
	\frac{1}{A'} cap(E,\Omega) \leq {\tt c} (E).
$$
\end{itemize}
Moreover, the above inequalities are sharp as far as the exponents are concerned.,
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the text says that its statements are provided by the following lemmas and remarks.

\begin{lem}[dominated by Bedford-Taylor capacity]  \label{lem:c-cap} Assume $\Om$ is strictly pseudoconvex. There exists a constant $A>0$ such that for every Borel set $E\subset\subset \Om$,
$$
	\tc(E) \leq A \left[cap(E,\Om)\right]^\frac{1}{n}.
$$
\end{lem}

\begin{lem} \label{lem:cap-c} Let $D \subset \subset \Om$. There exists  a constant $A'$ depending  only on $D$ and $\Om$ such that for every Borel set $E \subset D$,
$$	cap(E,\Om) \leq A'\, \tc(E)
$$
\end{lem}

\begin{remark}\label{rmk:inner-reg} The relative compactness condition of $E$ will be removed later after proving that $\tc(\cdot,\Om)$ is a capacity in the sense of Choquet (Theorem~\ref{thm:choquet-c}).
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Proof of the last statement in Theorem~\ref{thm:intro-cap-comp}]
\label{rmk:sharp-exp} The Alexander-Taylor inequality \cite{AT84} reads
$$
	\exp (-A_r/cap(K,\Om)) \leq T_R(K) \leq \exp(-2\pi/[cap(K,\Om)]^\frac{1}{n}),
$$
where the exponents in both inequalities are sharp (\cite[Remark~2]{AT84}).
Together with $cap(K) \leq A_r \tc(K)$ in Lemma~\ref{lem:cap-c}
and the first inequality in Lemma~\ref{lem:AT-type} we derive 
$$
	\exp (-A/cap(K,\Om)) \leq \exp (-A/\tc(K,\Om))    \leq \exp(-2\pi/[cap(K,\Om)]^\frac{1}{n})
$$
with the sharp exponents. So are the ones in Lemma~\ref{lem:c-cap} and Lemma~\ref{lem:cap-c}.
\end{remark}

In the local setting, “the key idea in the proof of the first inequality is making use of Cegrell's inequality \cite{Ce04}” while “the one for the second inequality comes from the comparison principle.” The “optimality of the exponents is extracted from the Alexander-Taylor type inequality which is proved in the following lemma.”

\begin{lem} \label{lem:AT-type} There exist positive constants $A_r$ and $A_R$ depending on $r,R$ respectively such that for every compact  set $K\subset B_r$,
$$ \exp(- A_R \; \tc (K,B_R)^{-1}) \leq  T_R(K) \leq 	\exp(- A_r\;\tc (K, B_R)^{-1/n}).
$$ 
Equivalently,
\[\label{eq:AT}
	\frac{1}{A_r} \frac{1}{M_K^n} \leq \tc (K,B_R) \leq \frac{A_R }{M_K}.
\]
\end{lem}

theorem:
\label{thm:main}
There exists a constant $c>0$ such that the following holds. Let $M$ be a closed hyperbolic 3-manifold and let $\gamma\subset M$ be a simple closed geodesic. Suppose that $\gamma$ has length $\ell$ and has an embedded tubular neighborhood of radius at least $R\in(0,1)$. Then the unique hyperbolic metric on $M-\gamma$ has volume
\[
{\rm vol}(M-\gamma)\le{\rm vol}(M)+c\frac{\ell}{R}.
\]
sketch:
To prove Theorem~\ref{thm:main}, the authors “follow the approach of \cite[Theorem 10.1]{AST} with a difference.” The steps described are:
\begin{itemize}
\item “We first change the metric of $M$ inside a tubular neighborhood $T$ of $\gamma$ interpolating between the metric on the tube and the metric on a suitable hyperbolic cusp.”
\item This is done “by keeping under control the scalar curvature (see Lemma~\ref{lem:functiondrill}).”
\item “Then we compare the volume of the modified metric with that of the hyperbolic structure on $M-\gamma$.”
\item “Instead of relying on Perelman's monotonicity formula,” they “exploit some other consequences of Perelman's work \cite{Pe1,Pe2}, namely, the exact computation of the sigma invariant of closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds as achieved by Anderson \cite{And} and Kleiner and Lott \cite{KL}.”
\item “This allows us to keep track of the fact that we \emph{did not change} the metric outside the tubular neighborhood $T$ and obtain an additive error rather than a multiplicative one in the volume increase.”
\end{itemize}

theorem:
[\cite{wagner}]
	\label{wagner-thm}
	If a graph $G$ is maximal $K_5$-minor-free, then $G$ can be built by a sequence of $2$-sums and 
	$3$-sums, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs and copies of $M_8$.
sketch:
To prove the Main Theorem, the authors “would like to reduce to the case of planar graphs and copies of $M_8$” using Wagner’s decomposition, but they note they “must ensure that [the] disjoint $\MM$-colorings $\vph_1,\vph_2,\vph_3$ agree on the copies of $K_2$ and $K_3$ where we perform our 2-sums and 3-sums,” which “motivates the following slightly stronger lemmas.” They “handle the copies of $M_8$ first, even for 6 disjoint $\MM$-colorings, since this step is easy”: for $M_8$ they “use induction on the number $n'$ of uncolored vertices,” with base case “$n'=0$” trivial, and the induction step because “$M_8$ is 3-degenerate, by Lemma~\ref{ext-lem} with $s:=6$ and $t:=6$,” yielding disjoint colorings that can be chosen to extend prescribed disjoint colorings on a specified $K_2$.

For planar graphs, they aim to prove Lemma~\ref{planar-lem} (extending prescribed disjoint $\MM$-colorings on a $K_3$), and state that “the key to the proof of Lemma~\ref{planar-lem} is proving a still stronger statement, given below in the Key Lemma, that more easily facilitates proof by induction,” using a “Thomassen-style” inductive method on plane near-triangulations with specified outer-face structure and list conditions.

mcq.question: Let $G$ be a maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph, meaning that $G$ has no $K_5$ minor and adding any missing edge to $G$ creates a $K_5$ minor. Here a $2$-sum or $3$-sum means identifying two graphs along a common clique of size $2$ or $3$, respectively; a maximal planar graph is a planar graph to which no edge can be added while preserving planarity; and $M_8$ denotes the $8$-vertex Möbius ladder. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the structure of $G$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of $2$-sums and $3$-sums, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of clique-sums of order at most $3$, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs and copies of $M_8$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of $3$-sums, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs and copies of $M_8$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Every maximal $K_5$-minor-free graph $G$ can be obtained by a sequence of $2$-sums, without deleting edges, starting from maximal planar graphs and copies of $M_8$.

theorem:
\label{thm:main} For all $p\ge 1$, 
\[\sup_{\substack{f\in \cS(\bbR^2)\\ \|f\|_{p} \le 1 } }\big\|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\bbR^2)} =\infty. \],
sketch:
In the range $2<p\le 4$ the theorem is obtained “as an immediate consequence of a more quantitative version” (Theorem~\ref{thm:kakeya}). The argument is to “test $V^I_2A$ on $L^p$-functions of exponential type,” i.e. $f\in E(\lambda)$ with $\widehat f$ supported in $\{\xi:|\xi|\le \lambda\}$, and to study the growth of
\[
B_p(\lambda)=\sup\{\|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p}:\ \|f\|_{L^p}\le 1,\ f\in E(\lambda)\}.
\]
While $B_p(\lambda)$ is finite (e.g. $\|V_2^IA f\|_p\lesssim \sup_{1\le t\le 2}\|\partial_t A_t f\|_p\lesssim \lambda\|f\|_p$ for $f\in E(\lambda)$), the quantification asserts a lower bound $B_p(\lambda)\ge c(\log\lambda)^{\frac12-\frac1p}$ for $p>2$ and large $\lambda$. In proving this quantitative bound, the authors “reduce matters to lower bounds for multipliers of the form $\chi(|\xi|-\lambda)$ for large $\lambda$, with nonnegative $\chi\in C_c^\infty(\mathbb R)$,” invoking that C\'ordoba showed (for $4/3\le p\le 4$) the corresponding convolution operator norms have logarithmic size and that this “matches the lower bound in the following lemma.”

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:aux2} There exist $\eps>0$, $\la_1=\la_1(p)>1$ and $c>0$  such that for all $\la>\la_1$ the following holds for $p>2$: 

For all nonnegative $L^\infty$ functions $u$ supported in $[-2\eps^2,2\eps^2]$ that are bounded below by $1$ in $[-\eps^2,\eps^2]$,  and all integers $n\geq 10$,
\Be\label{eq:lowerbd-Cord}\| u(|D|-2^{2n})\|_{L^p(\bbR^2)\to L^p(\bbR^2)}  \ge c  n^{\frac 12-\frac 1p}\,.
\Ee
\end{lemma}

mcq.question:
Let $p\ge 1$, let $\mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$ denote the Schwartz class on $\mathbb R^2$, and let $\|\cdot\|_p$ denote the usual $L^p(\mathbb R^2)$ norm. For $t>0$, define the circular averaging operators
\[
A_t f(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} f(x_1-t\cos\alpha,\,x_2-t\sin\alpha)\,d\alpha,
\]
and let $V_2^I A f$ denote the $2$-variation in the parameter $t$ over a fixed interval $I$ of the family $\{A_t f\}_{t\in I}$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $V_2^I A f$ acting on $f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For every $p\ge 1$, \[\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty.\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every $p>2$, 
\[
\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty,
\]
whereas for every $1\le p\le 2$ one has
\[
\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}<\infty.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every $p>2$, 
\[
\sup_{\substack{f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)\\ \|f\|_p\le 1}} \|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}=\infty.
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every $p>2$ there exist constants $C_p<\infty$ and $\lambda_0(p)\ge 1$ such that, whenever $\lambda\ge \lambda_0(p)$ and $f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$ satisfies $\|f\|_p\le 1$ and $\operatorname{supp}\widehat f\subseteq \{\xi\in\mathbb R^2:|\xi|\le \lambda\}$, one has
\[
\|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}\le C_p\,(\log \lambda)^{\frac14-\frac1{2p}}.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
Whenever $I$ is a fixed compact subinterval of $(0,\infty)$, the smooth dependence of $A_t f$ on $t$ implies that for every $p\ge 1$ there is a constant $C_{p,I}<\infty$ such that
\[
\|V_2^I A f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}\le C_{p,I}\,\|f\|_{L^p(\mathbb R^2)}
\quad\text{for all }f\in \mathcal S(\mathbb R^2).
\]

theorem:
[Unconditional exceedances and maximum edge length]\label{thm:uncond-max}
Consider the continuum model in which $V$ is a homogeneous Poisson point process on $\mathbb{R}^d$ with unit intensity, and let the connection probabilities be given by
\begin{equation}\label{eq:conn-prob-intro}
p_{xy}=1-\exp\!\left\{-\lambda\,\frac{W_xW_y}{|x-y|^\alpha}\right\},
\qquad \lambda>0,\ \alpha>0,
\end{equation}
For $n\in\mathbb{N}$ set $B_n=[-n,n]^d$ and define
\[
e_n^*:=\max_{\{x,y\}\in E_{n}} |x-y|,
\qquad
E_{n}:=\bigl\{\{x,y\}\in E:\ x\in V\cap B_n\bigr\},
\]
as well as the exceedance count $F(n,r)$ from
\begin{align}
F(n,r)
&:= \sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n^c}
\mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\, x\leftrightarrow y\}}
+ \frac12\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n}
\mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\, x\leftrightarrow y\}},
\label{eq:Fnr-intro}
\end{align}
Assume that the weight distribution satisfies $\mathbb{P}(W>w)=w^{-\beta}$ for $w\ge 1$, with tail index $\beta>0$, and that the
finite-degree condition
\begin{equation}\label{eq:finite-degree-cond-intro}
\min\{\alpha,\alpha\beta\}>d.
\end{equation}
holds.

\smallskip
For $r>0$ set $r_n=c_n r$ and define
\[
\theta=
\begin{cases}
\alpha\beta-d, & 0<\beta<1,\\
\alpha-d, & \beta\ge 1.
\end{cases}
\]
Then, in each of the regimes \textup{(a)}--\textup{(c)} below, with the corresponding choice of $c_n$, we have
\begin{equation*}\label{eq:pois-frechet-unified}
F(n,r)\ \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{d}\ \mathrm{Poisson}\!\bigl(r^{-\theta}\bigr),
\qquad
\mathbb{P}\!\left(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r\right)\ \longrightarrow\ \exp\!\bigl(-r^{-\theta}\bigr),
\qquad r>0,
\end{equation*}
that is, $c_n^{-1}e_n^*$ converges in distribution to a Fr\'echet random variable with parameter $\theta$.

\smallskip
\noindent\textup{(a) {Infinite-mean regime} $(0<\beta<1)$.}
Assume $\alpha\beta\in(d,2d]$ and set
\[
c_n=C_1\,n^{\frac{d}{\alpha\beta-d}}(\log n)^{\frac{1}{\alpha\beta-d}}.
\]

\smallskip
\noindent\textup{(b) {Borderline regime} $(\beta=1)$.}
Assume $\alpha\in(d,2d]$ and set
\[
c_n=C_2\,n^{\frac{d}{\alpha-d}}(\log n)^{\frac{2}{\alpha-d}}.
\]

\smallskip
\noindent\textup{(c) {Finite-mean regime} $(\beta>1)$.}
Assume $\alpha\in(d,2d)$ and set
\[
c_n=C_3\,n^{\frac{d}{\alpha-d}}.
\]
Here $C_1, C_2, C_3\in(0,\infty)$ denote explicit constants independent of $n$ (specified in the following corollary.

\begin{corollary}\label{cor:cn-choice}
Let $\theta:=\alpha\beta-d$ if $0<\beta<1$ and $\theta:=\alpha-d$ if $\beta\ge 1$. Define $c_n$ by
\[
c_n:=
\begin{cases}
\Bigl(2^d\omega_d\,\Gamma(1-\beta)\lambda^\beta\,\beta\,\dfrac{d}{\alpha\beta-d}\cdot \dfrac{\alpha d}{\theta}\Bigr)^{1/\theta}
\,n^{d/\theta}(\log n)^{1/\theta},
& 0<\beta<1,\\[2mm]
\Bigl(2^d\omega_d\,\dfrac12\lambda\,\dfrac{d}{\alpha-d}\cdot \dfrac{\alpha^2 d^2}{\theta^2}\Bigr)^{1/\theta}
\,n^{d/\theta}(\log n)^{2/\theta},
& \beta=1,\\[2mm]
\Bigl(2^d\omega_d\,\lambda\,\dfrac{\beta^2}{(\beta-1)^2}\,\dfrac{d}{\alpha-d}\Bigr)^{1/\theta}
\,n^{d/\theta},
& \beta>1,
\end{cases}
\]
and set $r_n=c_n r$ for fixed $r>0$. Then
\[
I_1 \sim r^{-\theta},
\qquad n\to\infty.
\]
\end{corollary}
sketch:
The post-theorem introduction only gives a high-level outline of the proof strategy (without detailed steps specific to each regime). To prove the main theorem, it says:

- “The Poisson approximation for the exceedance counts is established as follows. First we develop a Palm--coupling variant of Stein's method for Poisson functionals, extending ideas from Mathew Penrose, \emph{Random Geometric Graphs} (2003).”
- “Then, an essential input is a precise asymptotic evaluation of the mean number of exceedances in the relevant scaling regimes.”

It further indicates where the full proof is located: “Next we give the full proof of the main theorem; in particular, we state the Poisson approximation theorem based on a Palm--coupling approach and derive the corresponding exceedance limits.”

mcq.question:
In the continuum scale-free percolation model, let \(V\) be a unit-intensity homogeneous Poisson point process on \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Each \(x\in V\) carries an i.i.d. weight \(W_x\) with \(\mathbb{P}(W>w)=w^{-\beta}\) for \(w\ge 1\), and, conditional on \(V\) and the weights, distinct vertices \(x,y\) are connected independently with probability \(p_{xy}=1-\exp\{-\lambda W_xW_y/|x-y|^\alpha\}\), where \(|\cdot|\) is the Euclidean norm. Write \(E\) for the random edge set and \(x\leftrightarrow y\) for the event that \(x\) and \(y\) are joined by an edge. Define \(B_n=[-n,n]^d\), \(E_n=\{\{x,y\}\in E:x\in V\cap B_n\}\), \(e_n^*=\max_{\{x,y\}\in E_n}|x-y|\), and for fixed \(r>0\) let
\[
F(n,r)=\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n^c} \mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\ x\leftrightarrow y\}} + \frac12\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n} \mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\ x\leftrightarrow y\}},
\]
where \(r_n=c_n r\). Assume \(\min\{\alpha,\alpha\beta\}>d\) and either (a) \(0<\beta<1\) with \(\alpha\beta\in(d,2d]\) and \(c_n=C_1 n^{d/(\alpha\beta-d)}(\log n)^{1/(\alpha\beta-d)}\), or (b) \(\beta=1\) with \(\alpha\in(d,2d]\) and \(c_n=C_2 n^{d/(\alpha-d)}(\log n)^{2/(\alpha-d)}\), or (c) \(\beta>1\) with \(\alpha\in(d,2d)\) and \(c_n=C_3 n^{d/(\alpha-d)}\), where \(C_1,C_2,C_3>0\) are constants independent of \(n\). Writing \(\theta=\alpha\beta-d\) for \(0<\beta<1\) and \(\theta=\alpha-d\) for \(\beta\ge 1\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of \(F(n,r)\) and \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) as \(n\to\infty\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\), and \(\mathbb{P}(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r)\to \exp(-r^{-\theta})\), so \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) converges in distribution to a Fr\'echet random variable with parameter \(\theta\); however, in the borderline regime \(\beta=1\) this conclusion requires \(\alpha\in(d,2d)\), excluding the endpoint \(\alpha=2d\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(\kappa r^{-\theta}\) for some finite constant \(\kappa=\kappa(d,\alpha,\beta,\lambda)\in(0,\infty)\), and \(\mathbb{P}(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r)\to \exp(-\kappa r^{-\theta})\); equivalently, \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) has a Fr\'echet limit with parameter \(\theta\) but with a non-unit scale.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\), but because edges with both endpoints in \(B_n\) are counted with the factor \(1/2\), the event \(\{e_n^*\le r_n\}\) is not exactly the same as \(\{F(n,r)=0\}\); hence the Poisson limit for \(F(n,r)\) does not by itself yield the Fr\'echet limit for \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\).
