theorem:
[Main result]\label{thm:intro-main}
Let \(0<r<R\). Then
\[
\|C_{A(r,R)}\|_{L^2\to L^2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\mu_1^{ND}(r,R)}},
\]
where \(\mu_1^{ND}(r,R)\) is the first eigenvalue of
\[
-\Delta U=\mu U \ \text{in }A(r,R),\qquad
\partial_\nu U=0 \ \text{on }|z|=r,\qquad
U=0 \ \text{on }|z|=R.
\]
Equivalently, \(\mu_1^{ND}(r,R)=\kappa_1^2\), where \(\kappa_1\) is the smallest
positive root of a Bessel determinant, in establishing the main theorem.

We use the following theorem.
\begin{theorem}[Sharp \(L^2\)-norm on a circular annulus]\label{thm:norm-annulus}
Let \(0<r<R\), \(A=A(r,R)=\{z\in\C:\ r<|z|<R\}\), and
\[
(C_A f)(z)=\frac1\pi\int_A \frac{f(w)}{z-w}\,dA(w),\qquad f\in L^2(A).
\]
Then
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\kappa_1},
\]
where \(\kappa_1>0\) is the smallest positive solution of
\begin{equation}\label{eq:kappa1}
J_1(\kappa r)\,Y_0(\kappa R)-Y_1(\kappa r)\,J_0(\kappa R)=0 .
\end{equation}
\end{theorem}
sketch: The proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:intro-main} "exploits rotational symmetry": one performs a Fourier decomposition to reduce \(C_{A(r,R)}\) to "a family of one-dimensional weighted Hardy-type operators on radial profiles." Next, "a variational argument" produces a Sturm--Liouville problem "whose first eigenvalue controls the sharp operator norm." Finally, "the Bessel determinant arises from solving the radial eigenvalue equation explicitly." The "extremizers are given in terms of the corresponding mixed eigenfunction," highlighting "a structural distinction between simply and doubly connected domains."

mcq.question:
Let \(0<r<R\), let \(A=A(r,R)=\{z\in\mathbb{C}: r<|z|<R\}\), and define the Cauchy transform on the annulus by
\[
(C_A f)(z)=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_A \frac{f(w)}{z-w}\,dA(w),\qquad z\in A,\ f\in L^2(A),
\]
where \(dA\) denotes planar area measure. If \(J_\nu\) and \(Y_\nu\) denote the Bessel functions of the first and second kind, respectively, which statement about the \(L^2(A)\to L^2(A)\) operator norm of \(C_A\) holds?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\kappa_1},
\]
where \(\kappa_1>0\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_1(\kappa r)Y_0(\kappa R)-Y_1(\kappa r)J_0(\kappa R)=0.
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\kappa_1},
\]
where \(\kappa_1>0\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_0(\kappa r)Y_0(\kappa R)-Y_0(\kappa r)J_0(\kappa R)=0.
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
The norm can be written in the form
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\mu_1}},
\]
where \(\mu_1\) is the first eigenvalue of a mixed Neumann--Dirichlet Laplacian problem on the annulus; equivalently, \(\mu_1=\kappa_1^2\) for a smallest positive root \(\kappa_1\) of a Bessel transcendental equation depending on \(r\) and \(R\).

choices.label: D
choices.text:
The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\kappa_1},
\]
where \(\kappa_1>0\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_0(\kappa r)Y_1(\kappa R)-Y_0(\kappa r)J_1(\kappa R)=0.
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\lambda_1(A)}},
\]
where \(\lambda_1(A)\) is the first Dirichlet eigenvalue of \(-\Delta\) on \(A\); equivalently, \(\sqrt{\lambda_1(A)}\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_0(\kappa r)Y_0(\kappa R)-J_0(\kappa R)Y_0(\kappa r)=0.
\]

theorem:
Let $1<p<\infty$ and let $\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^N$ be an open set. Let us suppose that there exists $k\in\mathbb{N}\setminus\{0\}$ such that
\begin{equation}\
\label{PL}
\lambda_{k,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)<\mathcal{E}_p(\Omega).
\end{equation}
Then, for every $\ell\in\{1,\dots,k\}$ the minmax value $\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)$ is an eigenvalue of the Dirichlet $p-$Laplacian on $\Omega$. 
\par
Moreover, we have:
\begin{itemize}
\item if $\mathcal{E}_p(\Omega)<+\infty$,  for every $\ell\in\{1,\dots,k\}$ there exist two constants $C_\ell=C_\ell(N, p, \Omega)>0$ and $\alpha_\ell=\alpha_\ell(p,\Omega)>0$ such that for every eigenfunction $u_\ell\in W^{1,p}_0(\Omega)$ associated to $\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)$, we have
\[
|u_\ell(x)|\le C_\ell\,\|u_\ell\|_{L^p(\Omega)}\,e^{-\alpha_\ell\,|x|}\,,\qquad \text{for every}\ x\in\Omega;
\]
\item if $\mathcal{E}_p(\Omega)=+\infty$, for every $\ell\in\mathbb{N}\setminus\{0\}$ and every $\alpha>0$ there exists a constant $C_\ell=C_\ell(N, p,\Omega,\alpha)>0$ such that 
\[
|u_\ell(x)|\le C_\ell\,\|u_\ell\|_{L^p(\Omega)}\,e^{-\alpha\,|x|}\,,\qquad \text{for every}\ x\in\Omega.
\]
\end{itemize}
sketch:
For the \v{S}nol-Simon--type decay estimate in Theorem~\ref{PL}, the authors stress that their proof is “based on nonlinear techniques”: they “first obtain exponential decay in $L^p$ norm and then use an $L^\infty-L^p$ estimate ‘localized at infinity’ to transform it into a pointwise decay estimate.” The “decay in $L^p$ norm is obtained by combining Caccioppoli and Poincar\'e inequalities,” while the second step uses “a classical tool in the De Giorgi-Moser regularity theory for quasilinear equations.”

For the existence/eigenvalue part of the main theorem, they “use a \emph{spectral stability argument},” i.e. “prove that [the] minmax Ljusternik-Schnirelmann values can be approximated by the eigenvalues of a perturbed operator, having good compactness properties.” Concretely, they “\emph{compactify}” the problem by adding “a strongly confining potential $V_n$,” obtaining “infinitely many ‘perturbed’ eigenvalues, by the usual minmax procedure.” If $V_n\to0$ suitably, these perturbed eigenvalues “converge to the original minmax Ljusternik-Schnirelmann values.” To show that limits below $\mathcal{E}_p(\Omega)$ yield actual eigenvalues, they seek “strong convergence in $L^p$ of the ‘perturbed’ eigenfunctions,” using that these enjoy a “uniform \v{S}nol-Simon decay estimate” when the corresponding perturbed eigenvalues converge to some $\lambda_{k,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)<\mathcal{E}_p(\Omega)$. This “uniform fall-off… implies an $L^p$ equi-tightness property,” allowing the “Riesz-Fr\'echet-Kolmogorov compactness criterion”; “in a nutshell, we obtain compactness through regularity.” They also describe this as “essentially a $\Gamma$-convergence--type argument in disguise, but used for higher critical points.”

mcq.question:
Let $1<p<\infty$ and let $\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^N$ be open. Assume that there exists $k\in\mathbb{N}\setminus\{0\}$ such that
\[
\lambda_{k,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)<\mathcal{E}_p(\Omega).
\]
Here $\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)$ denotes the $\ell$-th Lusternik--Schnirelmann minmax value, and saying that a number $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of the Dirichlet $p$-Laplacian on $\Omega$ means that there exists a nonzero $u\in W^{1,p}_0(\Omega)$ such that
\[
-\operatorname{div}\big(|\nabla u|^{p-2}\nabla u\big)=\lambda |u|^{p-2}u
\]
in the weak sense on $\Omega$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the minmax values $\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)$ under these assumptions?

sketch:
In establishing the main theorem, the proof is based on “the construction of a specific set, denoted by $\Lambda(\Omega)$, associated with the geometry of $\Omega$ and the positive flow generated by
\begin{equation}\label{con}
\begin{cases}\nu_t - \Delta u = 0, & (x,t)\in \Omega\times\mathbb{R}^+, \\
\partial_{\nu} u = \lambda g(u), & (x,t)\in \partial\Omega\times\mathbb{R}^+, \\
u(x,0)=u_0(x), & x\in \Omega,
\end{cases}
\end{equation}
.” The strategy is that “whenever $\Lambda(\Omega)$ is nonempty, it contains the desired pattern,” so “the core of the argument consists in proving that $\Lambda(\Omega)\neq\emptyset$.”

We first prove the following theorem.
\begin{theorem}\label{tmi}
Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a bounded convex domain and again let
$
D := D(\Omega).
$
Then there exists a constant $c>0$ depending only on the dimension such that
\[
\mu_1(\Omega) \ge \frac{c}{D}.
\]
\end{theorem}
This estimate plays a key role in the proof of the main theorem.

mcq.question:
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^2\) be a smooth bounded convex domain with inradius \(\operatorname{inr}(\Omega)=r\) (the radius of the largest ball contained in \(\Omega\)) and diameter \(D(\Omega)=D\). Consider the parabolic boundary-value problem with bistable boundary nonlinearity \(g(u)=u-u^3\):
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t-\Delta u=0, & (x,t)\in \Omega\times \mathbb{R}^+,\\
\partial_{\nu}u=\lambda g(u), & (x,t)\in \partial\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^+,\\
u(x,0)=u_0(x), & x\in \Omega,
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\partial_{\nu}u\) denotes the outward normal derivative on \(\partial\Omega\), and a "pattern" means a non-constant stable stationary solution of this problem. Assuming the diameter \(D\) is sufficiently large, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of patterns for this problem?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\) such that the problem admits a pattern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\), depending only on \(r\), such that every smooth bounded convex domain with inradius \(r\) and sufficiently large diameter admits a pattern for that same \(\lambda\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\) such that the problem admits a non-constant stationary solution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\), depending only on \(r\) and \(D\), such that every smooth bounded convex domain with \(\operatorname{inr}(\Omega)=r\) and \(D(\Omega)=D\) admits a pattern for that \(\lambda\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: One may choose \(\lambda\) so that \(0<\lambda\le \mu_1(\Omega)\), where \(\mu_1(\Omega)\) is the first positive Steklov eigenvalue of \(\Omega\), and then the problem admits a pattern.

mcq.question:
Let \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) be two smooth connected \(n\)-dimensional Riemannian manifolds with boundary. Let \(\omega_1\subset \mathcal M_1\) and \(\omega_2\subset \mathcal M_2\) be open connected subsets. For each \(j=1,2\), let \(L^2\Omega^1(\omega_j)\) denote the space of square-integrable 1-forms on \(\omega_j)\), and define the local source-to-final value map
\[
\mathcal N_j:\mathbb B_\delta\to L^2\Omega^1(\omega_j),\qquad \mathcal N_j(\mathbf f)=\mathbf u_{\mathbf f}(T,\cdot)|_{\omega_j},
\]
where \(\mathbb B_\delta\) is a small ball of admissible source 1-forms, and \(\mathbf u_{\mathbf f}\) is the corresponding solution of the geometric Navier--Stokes system on \([0,T]\times \mathcal M_j\) with zero initial and boundary data, evaluated at the final time \(T\). Assume there exists a diffeomorphism \(\Phi:\omega_1\to\omega_2\) such that, with \(\Phi^*\) denoting pullback on the relevant source and final-value 1-forms,
\[
\mathcal N_1\,\Phi^*=\Phi^*\,\mathcal N_2.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The intertwining identity implies equality, via \(\Phi\), of the localized spectral projections for the stationary Stokes operator on \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\); however, this determines at most the domains of influence of the observation sets up to time \(T\), not the full manifolds up to isometry.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The intertwining identity already forces the first-order linearizations of \(\mathcal N_1\) and \(\mathcal N_2\) at \(0\) to agree under \(\Phi\); equivalently, the local source-to-final maps for the corresponding linearized Stokes systems coincide on the observation regions, which is weaker than global isometry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a sufficiently small \(\delta>0\), depending only on \(T\), such that intertwining on \(\mathbb B_\delta\) identifies the linearized Stokes dynamics on \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\); to deduce that \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) are globally isometric one would need the additional hypothesis that \(T\) exceed the relevant geometric controllability time.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The diffeomorphism \(\Phi\) extends to a global isometry \(\widetilde\Phi:\mathcal M_1\to\mathcal M_2\), and after identifying the manifolds by \(\widetilde\Phi\) the full nonlinear source-to-final value maps agree for all admissible \(L^2\)-source 1-forms, not merely for sources in the small ball \(\mathbb B_\delta\).

Let $2\le p<\infty$, $0<s<1$, and $0\le \Lambda\le 1$. Assume $\Omega\subset \mathbb H^n$ is a bounded open set and $u\in HW^{1,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)\cap L^{p-1}_{sp}(\mathbb H^n)$ is a weak solution of

\[

theorem:
[Fundamental inequality]\label{thm:main_intro}
Let $F\subset S^3$ be a closed surface. Then every knot $K\subset S^3$ satisfies:
\[
c(K;F) \ge 2\bigl(t(K)-\delta(F)\bigr)+1,
\]
where $t(K)$ is the tunnel number of $K$, and $\delta(F)=g(M_1)+g(M_2)-g(F)$ is the Heegaard deficiency of $F$.
sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the argument “relies on a chain of inequalities relating the surface crossing number $c(K;F)$, the surface ascending number $a(K;F)$, and the surface bridge number $b(K;F)$”:
\[
\frac{c(K;F)-1}{2} \ge a(K;F) \ge b(K;F)-1 \ge t(K)-\delta(F).
\]
This sequence yields the stated lower bound for $c(K;F)$ in terms of “a combination of the knot exterior's complexity, $t(K)$, and the ambient surface's Heegaard deficiency, $\delta(F)$.”

For optimality, the introduction says they “prove that the linear lower bound in the main theorem is asymptotically optimal” by taking iterated connected sums $K_m$ of a prime knot and obtaining
\[
c(K_m;F) \ge 2m - 2\delta(F) + 1.
\]
“Pairing this with a general upper bound derived from the subadditivity of the planar crossing number yields $c(K_m;F)=\Theta(t(K_m))$,” so “no general lower bound of higher order exists,” and $c(K;F)$ grows linearly with tunnel number.

\]
where \(g(M_i)\) denotes the Heegaard genus of \(M_i\) and \(g(F)\) denotes the genus of \(F\). For a knot \(K\subset S^3\), let \(c(K;F)\) denote the surface crossing number of \(K\) with respect to \(F\), i.e. the minimal number of crossings among all regular diagrams of \(K\) obtained by isotoping \(K\) into a regular neighborhood of \(F\). theorem:
\label{thm:intro}
The group of homeomorphisms of the real projective plane $\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^2$ and Möbius strip $M$ which are isotopic to the identity is stably unbounded with respect to commutator length in the sense of \cite{BIP}.
sketch: To prove the main theorem, the introduction says the "central tool is Bavard duality" (linking commutator length to the existence of "non-trivial homogeneous quasi-morphisms"). The strategy follows the quasi-morphism constructions of Brooks and their generalisation by Bestvina--Fujiwara via actions on hyperbolic spaces, and the analogous approach of Bowden--Hensel--Webb using the action of $\operatorname{Homeo}_0$ on a (fine) curve graph. Concretely, the paper is organised to (1) combine results of \cite{kuno-hyperbolicity} and \cite{BHW1} to show the Möbius strip and $\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^2$ have "hyperbolic and unbounded fine curve graphs", and then (2) use that result together with the methods of \cite{BHW2} to prove that both surfaces have "stably unbounded $\operatorname{Homeo}_0$" (hence stably unbounded commutator length).

mcq.question: Let \(\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)\) denote the group of homeomorphisms of a surface \(S\) that are isotopic to the identity. For \(G=\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)\), let \([G,G]\) denote its commutator subgroup, and for \(g\in [G,G]\), let \(\operatorname{cl}_G(g)\) be the commutator length of \(g\), i.e. the least number of commutators in \(G\) whose product is \(g\). For \(S\in\{\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^2, M\}\), where \(M\) is the Möbius strip, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about commutator length on \([\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S),\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)]\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For each \(S\in\{\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^2, M\}\), the group \(\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)\) is stably unbounded with respect to commutator length: there exists \(g\in [\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S),\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)]\) such that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \operatorname{cl}_{\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)}(g^n)/n>0\) (equivalently, some element has positive stable commutator length).

choices.label: B
choices.text: For each \(S\in\{\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^2, M\}\), there exists \(g\in [\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S),\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)]\) such that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \operatorname{cl}_{\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)}(g^n)/n=\infty\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: For each \(S\in\{\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^2, M\}\), the commutator length on \([\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S),\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)]\) is unbounded: for every \(N\) there exists \(g\in [\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S),\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)]\) with \(\operatorname{cl}_{\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)}(g)\ge N\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For each \(S\in\{\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^2, M\}\), commutator length on \([\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S),\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)]\) is unbounded, but stable commutator length vanishes identically: for every \(g\in [\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S),\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)]\), one has \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \operatorname{cl}_{\mathrm{Homeo}_0(S)}(g^n)/n=0\).

They justify these are vertices via an inductive condition and an inequality that “follows if $k\le N^{1/3}$,” and record that with $N_1=\lfloor N^{1/3}\rfloor$ and $N_2=\lceil N/N_1\rceil$ the points $(N_1,N_2)$ and $(N_2,N_1)$ are vertices. mcq.question:
Let \(N\) be a positive integer, and define
\[
H_N=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{R}^2: x,y>0,\ xy\ge N\}.
\]
Let
\[
I(H_N)=\operatorname{conv}(\mathbb{Z}^2\cap H_N)
\]
be the integer hull of \(H_N\), and let \(f_0(I(H_N))\) denote the number of vertices of \(I(H_N)\). Interpreting Vinogradov notation \(A \ll B\) to mean that \(A\) is bounded above by a constant multiple of \(B\) for all sufficiently large \(N\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of \(f_0(I(H_N))\) as \(N \to \infty\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \asymp N^{1/3}\log N\); equivalently, there exist constants \(c, C > 0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(c\,N^{1/3}\log N \le f_0(I(H_N)) \le C\,N^{1/3}\log N\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \asymp N^{1/3}\); equivalently, there exist constants \(c, C > 0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(c\,N^{1/3} \le f_0(I(H_N)) \le C\,N^{1/3}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \ll N^{1/3}\log N\); equivalently, there exists a constant \(C>0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(f_0(I(H_N))\le C\,N^{1/3}\log N\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N)) \asymp N^{1/3}\log N\log\log N\); equivalently, there exist constants \(c, C > 0\), independent of \(N\), such that for all sufficiently large \(N\), \(c\,N^{1/3}\log N\log\log N \le f_0(I(H_N)) \le C\,N^{1/3}\log N\log\log N\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(f_0(I(H_N))\) satisfies \(N^{1/3} \ll f_0(I(H_N)) \ll N^{1/3}\log N\), and these bounds are best possible up to multiplicative constants.

For $T>0$, let $u^\varepsilon$ be the corresponding weak solution on $\mathbb{R}^2\times(0,T)$. Let $\Phi^\varepsilon$ denote the cutoff interaction kernel, and write spatial convolution as
\[
(\Phi^\varepsilon*u^\varepsilon)(x,t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^2}\Phi^\varepsilon(x-y)u^\varepsilon(y,t)\,dy.
\]
For $1\le i\le N$, let $X_{N,i}^\varepsilon$ and $\overline X_i^\varepsilon$ solve, with the same $\mathbb{R}^2$-valued Brownian motions $B_i$ and the same initial data $\zeta_i$,
\[
dX_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)=\Big(2\exp\Big(-\frac1N\sum_{j=1}^N\Phi^\varepsilon(X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)-X_{N,j}^\varepsilon(t))\Big)+2\Big)^{1/2}dB_i(t),\qquad X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(0)=\zeta_i,
\]
and
\[
d\overline X_i^\varepsilon(t)=\big(2\exp(-\Phi^\varepsilon*u^\varepsilon(\overline X_i^\varepsilon,t))+2\big)^{1/2}dB_i(t),\qquad \overline X_i^\varepsilon(0)=\zeta_i.
\]
Fix $m,N\in\mathbb N^+$ and parameters $0<\theta<\tfrac12$, $0<\alpha<\tfrac\theta2$, and $m>\tfrac{\theta+1}{1-2\theta}$. Under the cutoff scaling $\varepsilon\sim N^{-\gamma}$, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative discrepancy between the particle system and the mean-field system?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
Assume the cutoff parameter is chosen at the boundary value
\[
\gamma=\min\Big\{\frac{\alpha}{3},\frac{-2\alpha+m(1-2\theta)-1}{4m+4}\Big\},\qquad \varepsilon\sim N^{-\gamma}.
\]
Then there exist $C(m,T)>0$ and $\eta>0$ satisfying
\[

such that, for every $t\in[0,T]$,

\mathbb P\Big(\max_{1\le i\le N}\big|X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)-\overline X_i^\varepsilon(t)\big|>N^{-\alpha}\Big)\le C(m,T)N^{-\eta}.
\]

mcq.question:
Let \(\mathcal C\) be a block-stable class of connected graphs, and let \(\mathcal B\) be the class of its 2-connected components (blocks). Write \(B(y)\) for the exponential generating function of \(\mathcal B\), let \(\rho_B\) be the radius of convergence of \(B\), and define
\[
u_C:=\frac{1}{\rho_B B''(\rho_B)}.
\]
Let \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) be the exponential generating function of connected rooted graphs in \(\mathcal C\), where \(x\) marks vertices and \(u\) marks blocks. For fixed \(u>0\), let \(\mathbb P_\nu\) denote the fixed-size block-weighted law under which a rooted graph \(G\in\mathcal C^{\bullet}\) with \(n\) vertices is sampled with probability proportional to \(u^{b(G)}\), where \(b(G)\) is the number of blocks of \(G\). The block tree of a sampled graph is the tree recording how its blocks are attached. Which statement holds for every such class \(\mathcal C\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, and the block tree of a graph sampled according to \(\mathbb P_\nu\) has a subcritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u<u_C\), while it has a critical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u\ge u_C\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, and the block tree of a graph sampled according to \(\mathbb P_\nu\) has a subcritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u<u_C\), a critical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law only at \(u=u_C\), and a supercritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u>u_C\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): in particular, the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The behaviour changes at the threshold \(u_C=1/(\rho_B B''(\rho_B))\): the singular behaviour of \(C^{\bullet}(x,u)\) depends on whether \(u<u_C\) or not, and under \(\mathbb P_\nu\) it is the decorated block tree itself that has a subcritical Bienaymé–Galton–Watson law when \(u<u_C\) and a critical one when \(u\ge u_C\), whereas the underlying undecorated block tree need not be Bienaymé–Galton–Watson.

theorem:
\label{maintheore}
Let $U \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ be a (possibly unbounded) open set with $d \geq 2$. Assume that $\mathbf{G} \in L^2_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $c \in L^1_{loc}(U)$. Let $A = (a_{ij})_{1 \leq i,j \leq d}$ be a symmetric matrix of functions such that $a_{ij} \in VMO_{loc}(U)$ for all $1 \leq i,j \leq d$, where $VMO_{loc}(U)$ is defined as follows.

\begin{defn} \label{defnvmo}
Let $\omega$ be a positive continuous function on $[0,\infty)$ with $\omega(0) = 0$. The space $VMO_\omega$ consists of all functions $g \in L^1_{loc}(\mathbb{R}^d)$ such that
\[
\sup_{z \in \mathbb{R}^d,\, r < R} \, r^{-2d} \int_{B_r(z)} \int_{B_r(z)} |g(x) - g(y)| \, dx \, dy \leq \omega(R) \quad \text{for all } R \in (0,\infty).
\]
Let $B$ be an open ball in $\mathbb{R}^d$. We define $VMO(B)$ as the set of all functions $f \in L^1(B)$ for which there exists an extension $\tilde{f} \in VMO_{\omega}$  (for some modulus $\omega$) such that $\tilde{f}|_B = f$. For an open set $U \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, the space $VMO_{loc}(U)$ is defined as the collection of all functions $f \in L^1_{loc}(U)$ such that $f \in VMO(B)$ for every open ball $B \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ with $\overline{B} \subset U$.
\end{defn}

Assume moreover that ${\rm div} A \in L^2_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$, where the meaning of ${\rm div}$ for a matrix field is as follows.

\begin{defn} \label{defndivmat}
Let $U$ be an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$, $\mathbf{E} = (e_1, \dots, e_d) \in L^1_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $B = (b_{ij})_{1 \leq i,j \leq d}$ be a (possibly non-symmetric) matrix of functions with $b_{ij} \in L^1_{loc}(U)$ for all $1 \leq i,j \leq d$. Let $\mathbf{F} \in L^1_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $f \in L^1_{loc}(U)$.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(i)] We say that ${\rm div} B = \mathbf{E}$ if
\[
\int_U \sum_{i,j=1}^d b_{ij} \, \partial_i \phi_j \, dx = - \int_U \sum_{j=1}^d e_j \, \phi_j \, dx \quad \text{for all } (\phi_1, \dots, \phi_d) \in C_0^\infty(U)^d.
\]
\item[(ii)] We say that ${\rm div} \mathbf{F} = f$ if
\[
\int_U \langle \mathbf{F}, \nabla \varphi \rangle \, dx = - \int_U f \varphi \, dx \quad \text{for all } \varphi \in C_0^\infty(U).
\]
\end{itemize}
\end{defn}

Also assume that $A$ is locally uniformly strictly elliptic and bounded on $U$, i.e. for every open ball $V$ with $\overline{V} \subset U$, there exist constants $\lambda_V, M_V > 0$ such that
\begin{equation} \label{locellst}
\lambda_V \| \xi \|^2 \leq \langle A(x) \xi, \xi \rangle, \quad \;\; \max_{1 \leq i,j \leq d} |a_{ij}(x)| \leq M_V
\quad \text{for a.e. } x \in V \text{ and all } \xi \in \mathbb{R}^d.
\end{equation}
Let $\tilde{\mathbf{F}} \in L^2_{loc}(U, \mathbb{R}^d)$ and $\tilde{f} \in L^1_{loc}(U)$. Suppose that $h \in L^{\infty}_{loc}(U)$ satisfies
\[
\int_U \left({\rm trace}(A \nabla^2 \varphi) + \langle \mathbf{G}, \nabla \varphi \rangle + c \varphi \right) h \, dx 
= \int_U \tilde{f} \varphi \, dx + \int_U \langle \tilde{\mathbf{F}}, \nabla \varphi \rangle \, dx \quad \text{for all } \varphi \in C_0^{\infty}(U).
\]
Then, in establishing the main theorem, $h \in H^{1,2}_{loc}(U).
sketch: To prove the main theorem, the paper’s stated strategy is to (i) “construct a constant $\lambda_0>0$ and a sub-Markovian resolvent $(R_\alpha)_{\alpha\ge \lambda_0}$ associated with the principal elliptic operator $L^A={\rm trace}(A\nabla^2)$”, and then (ii) “show that the function $h$ can be represented as the local weak $H^{1,2}$-limit of the family $(\alpha R_\alpha h)_{\alpha\ge \lambda_0}$ as $\alpha\to\infty$.” The introduction emphasizes that the proof relies on “resolvent estimates under the weaker assumptions that the coefficients belong to $VMO_{loc}(\mathbb{R}^d)$ and that ${\rm div}A\in L^2_{loc}(\mathbb{R}^d,\mathbb{R}^d)$, which enable us to derive the main result.”

mcq.question:
Let U \subset \mathbb{R}^d be an open set, possibly unbounded, with d \ge 2. Let \mathbf{G} \in L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d) and c \in L^1_{loc}(U). Let A=(a_{ij})_{1\le i,j\le d} be a symmetric matrix field such that each coefficient a_{ij} belongs to VMO_{loc}(U), meaning it has vanishing mean oscillation on every ball whose closure is contained in U. Assume also that the distributional divergence of A belongs to L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d), where \operatorname{div}A=\mathbf E means
\[
\int_U \sum_{i,j=1}^d a_{ij}\,\partial_i \psi_j\,dx=-\int_U \sum_{j=1}^d E_j\,\psi_j\,dx
\qquad \text{for all } (\psi_1,\dots,\psi_d)\in C_c^\infty(U)^d.
\]
Assume further that A is locally uniformly strictly elliptic and locally bounded on U: for every open ball V with \overline V\subset U, there exist constants \lambda_V,M_V>0 such that
\[
\lambda_V|\xi|^2\le \langle A(x)\xi,\xi\rangle,\qquad \max_{i,j}|a_{ij}(x)|\le M_V
\]
for a.e. x\in V and all \xi\in\mathbb{R}^d. Let \tilde{\mathbf F}\in L^2_{loc}(U,\mathbb{R}^d) and \tilde f\in L^1_{loc}(U). Suppose h\in L^\infty_{loc}(U) satisfies
\[
\int_U \big(\operatorname{trace}(A\nabla^2\varphi)+\langle \mathbf G,\nabla\varphi\rangle + c\varphi\big)h\,dx
= \int_U \tilde f\,\varphi\,dx + \int_U \langle \tilde{\mathbf F},\nabla\varphi\rangle\,dx
\qquad \text{for all }\varphi\in C_c^\infty(U),
\]
where \operatorname{trace}(A\nabla^2\varphi)=\sum_{i,j=1}^d a_{ij}\,\partial_{ij}\varphi. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every such h?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: h belongs to H^{1,2}_{loc}(U).

choices.label: B
choices.text: h belongs to H^{1,2}_{loc}(U), and for every open ball V with \overline V\subset U there exists a constant C_V depending only on the local coefficients and data such that \|h\|_{H^{1,2}(V)}\le C_V\big(\|\tilde{\mathbf F}\|_{L^2(V)}+\|\tilde f\|_{L^1(V)}\big).

choices.label: C
choices.text: h belongs to L^2_{loc}(U).

choices.label: D
choices.text: h has a representative that is locally Hölder continuous on U.

choices.label: E
choices.text: h belongs to H^{2,2}_{loc}(U).

sketch:
This paper states that it "employs the methods of contour integration and residue computation to prove the main conjecture." The approach is based on the contour-integration framework of Flajolet--Salvy and, specifically, on considering the two vanishing contour integrals
\[
\oint_{(\infty)} \frac{\Phi(s;x)\,\Li_{k_1,\ldots,k_r}(x_1,\ldots,x_r;s+1)}{s^q}\,ds=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\oint_{(\infty)} \frac{\Phi(s;x)\,\Li_{k_1,\ldots,k_r}(x_1,\ldots,x_r;1+a_1+s,\ldots,1+a_r+s)}{(s+a)^q}\,ds=0,
\]
where \(\Phi(s;x)\) is an "extended trigonometric function" defined by
\(\Phi(s;x):=\phi(s;x)-\phi(-s;x^{-1})-1/s\) with \(\phi(s;x)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty x^k/(k+s)\).
The paper then indicates the proof organization: next it provides "Laurent expansions of the integrands ... at their poles, preparing for the residue calculations," and later it gives the proofs of the main theorems via these residue computations, yielding the desired symmetry (modulo lower-depth product terms) and "a more generalized form along with explicit formulas."

theorem:
\label{thm:DKS2} \cite{DKS2} Let $M=S^2(q_1/p_1,q_2,p_2,q_3/p_3)$ be a Seifert manifold and assume $e(M)\neq 0.$ Then 
	$$|X(M)|=p_1^+ p_2^+ p_3^-  + p_1^- p_2^- p_3^- + \frac{1}{2}(|H_1(M,\Z)| +|H_1(M,\Z/2\Z)|)-x_M$$
	where $p_i^+=\lceil \frac{p_i}{2} \rceil-1$ and $p_i^-=\lfloor \frac{p_i}{2} \rfloor$ and $x_M$ is the number of exceptional abelian characters. Moreover, a point $[\rho]$ in $X(M)$ is reduced, except possibly if $[\rho]$ is an exceptional abelian character.,

mcq.question:
Let \(M=S^2(q_1/p_1,q_2/p_2,q_3/p_3)\) be a Seifert manifold with \(e(M)\neq 0\). Let
\[X(M)=\operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1(M),\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C))//\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)\]
denote the \(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb C)\)-character variety of \(M\). For \(i=1,2,3\), define \(p_i^+=\lceil p_i/2\rceil-1\) and \(p_i^-=\lfloor p_i/2\rfloor\), and let \(x_M\) denote the number of exceptional abelian characters. Writing \(|A|\) for the cardinality of a finite set or group \(A\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(X(M)\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: \[|X(M)|=p_1^+p_2^-p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^+p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr)-x_M.\]

choices.label: C
choices.text: \[|X(M)|\le p_1^+p_2^+p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^-p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr).\]

choices.label: D
choices.text: \[|X(M)|=p_1^+p_2^+p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^-p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr)-2x_M.\]

choices.label: E
choices.text: \[|X(M)|=p_1^+p_2^+p_3^-+p_1^-p_2^-p_3^-+\frac{1}{2}\bigl(|H_1(M,\mathbb Z)|+|H_1(M,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)|\bigr)-x_M,\]\text{ and }[\rho]\in X(M)\text{ is reduced if and only if }[\rho]\text{ is not an exceptional abelian character.}

sketch:
For $n=2$, the previously used argument (\cite[Lemma 2, Lemma 4]{MOW25}) identified the law of $R:=\frac{1}{2}{\rm tr}\,F^*F$ via the one-dimensional It\^o SDE
\[
 dR=R_{\tau}\,d\tau+\sqrt{R_{\tau}^2-1}\,dw,\qquad R_{\tau=0}=1,
\]
which yields the needed moment information.

In general dimension $n$, by $\mathbf O(n)$-invariance “one obtains an evolution for the $(n-1)$ quantities ${\rm tr}F^*F,\,{\rm tr}(F^*F)^2,\dots,{\rm tr}(F^*F)^{n-1}$,” but the authors state they are “unable to leverage this evolution in order to characterize the evolution of $R$.”

Instead, they “take a different route”: they use that “$\mathbb E\,{\rm tr}(F^*F)^2$ and $\mathbb E\,{\rm tr}^2 F^*F$ satisfy a linear system of ODEs which is closed in any dimension $n$ and can be solved explicitly.” From this explicit solution, they “learn that $\mathbb E\,{\rm tr}(F^*F)^2$ can be well approximated by $\mathbb E\,{\rm tr}^2 F^*F$,” which “in turn allows us to approximately close the equation for $R$ in dimensions $n>2$.”,

mcq.question:
Let \(F_\tau\) be the matrix-valued process solving the Stratonovich SDE
\[
dF_\tau = F_\tau \circ dB_\tau, \qquad F_0=I,
\]
where \(B_\tau\) is a Brownian motion on \(\mathfrak{sl}(n):=\{A:\operatorname{tr}A=0\}\). Define the squared Frobenius norm by \(|F|^2:=\operatorname{tr}(F^*F)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}\) for every integer \(p\ge 1\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every integer \(p\ge 1\), the \(2p\)-th moment of the Frobenius norm satisfies
\[
 n\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+1}\right)\tau\right)
 \le \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 \le n^{p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+1}\right)\tau\right).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
For every integer \(p\ge 1\), there exist constants \(c_{n,p},C_{n,p}>0\) such that
\[
 c_{n,p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
 \le \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 \le C_{n,p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
\]
for all \(\tau\ge 0\).

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every integer \(p\ge 1\), the \(2p\)-th moment of the Frobenius norm satisfies
\[
 n\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
 \le \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 \le n^{p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+3}\right)\tau\right).
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every integer \(p\ge 1\), the \(2p\)-th moment of the Frobenius norm is given exactly by
\[
 \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 = n^{p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
\]
for all \(\tau\ge 0\).

sketch:
To prove the main theorem, we start from Nikulin’s explicit description of the exceptional lattice (Nikulin, “Elliptic fibrations on K3 surfaces”, Theorem~4.2), see also the following proposition.

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:exceptionalIntersection}
Let $L$ be a lattice such that $\mathcal E_\infty(L)$ is non-empty. 
Then the exceptional lattice satisfies
\begin{align*}
    E(L)=\bigcap _{e\in \mathcal E_{\infty}(L)} (e^{\perp})^{(2)}_{\mathrm{pr}}.
\end{align*}
\end{proposition}

It implies that $E(L)$ is trivial “as soon as every simple $(-2)$-root $r\in L$ admits a cusp with infinite stabilizer $e\in \overline{\mathcal{D}}_L$ such that $e.r>0$.”

For a hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$, one “tak[es] an arbitrary maximal overlattice of $L$” to reduce to lattices of the form
\[
L = U\oplus E_8^n\oplus M,\quad n\ge 5,\ \rk(M)\le 11
\]
by the following proposition.

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:structure}
\tIf $L$ is a hyperbolic lattice of rank at least $6$, then there exist an overlattice 
    $L'$ of $L$, an integer $n$ and a negative-definite lattice $M$ such that
    $$L'\cong U\oplus E_{8}^{n}\oplus M,$$
    with $\rk M\le 11$ and such that the root part of $M$ does not contain any $E_{8}$ or an ADE lattice of rank at least $9$.
\end{proposition}

Using the isometry $U\oplus E_8^3\cong U\oplus \Lambda$ and that the Leech lattice $\Lambda$ has no $(-2)$-root, one deduces that “the exceptional lattice $E(L)$ is contained in $M$.” Since there are “only finitely many possibilities for the root part of $M$,” the argument given later checks all cases and shows “that $E(L)=0$ for all of them,” which “conclud[es] the proof of the main theorem.”

mcq.question: Here, a hyperbolic lattice means an even integral lattice of signature $(1,\operatorname{rk} L-1)$, and the exceptional lattice of $L$ is the sublattice of vectors in $L$ with finite orbit under $\mathrm{O}^+(L)/W(L)$, where $\mathrm{O}^+(L)$ is the group of positive isometries of $L$ and $W(L)$ is the subgroup generated by reflections in $(-2)$-roots. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the exceptional lattice of every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$, the lattice $L$ itself is isometric to $U\oplus E_8^n\oplus M$ for some $n\ge 5$ and some negative-definite lattice $M$ of rank at most $11$ whose root part contains no $E_8$ or ADE summand of rank at least $9$.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The exceptional lattice of every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$ has rank at most $11$.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$, if $L'$ is a maximal overlattice of $L$ with $L'\cong U\oplus E_8^n\oplus M$ and $n\ge 5$, then the exceptional lattice of $L$ is exactly the root sublattice of $M$.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$, after passing to a maximal overlattice $L'\cong U\oplus E_8^n\oplus M$ with $n\ge 5$, the isometry $U\oplus E_8^3\cong U\oplus\Lambda$ with the Leech lattice $\Lambda$ already forces the exceptional lattice of $L$ to be trivial, without any further case-by-case analysis of $M$.
